descriptionEnglish: Facilitates the purchase, sale, or rental of houses, apartments, industrial buildings, etc. Markets and shows properties.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Employment Agency assesses that there will be competition for jobs for real estate agents in the coming year. This means that individuals with the necessary education or experience will have small to medium opportunities for work, but depending on the focus within the profession, the labor market may vary.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: In the two-year outlook, the Employment Agency assesses that job opportunities for real estate agents will be small to medium. Opportunities in the profession increase if you have relevant certifications, qualifications, or professional licenses.
forecast.ingress: Det finns cirka 4900 yrkesverksamma fastighetsmäklare i Sverige, varav cirka 51 procent är kvinnor och 49 procent är män.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Real estate agents primarily work with cognitive and digital tasks: valuation, marketing, client contact, documentation, advertisements, comparisons of objects, and administrative processes. AI can already support large parts of this through text generation, lead responses, price analysis, image/advertisement optimization, and meeting/document support. At the same time, the profession requires a lot of personal selling, trust-building, on-site viewings, negotiations, and local market knowledge, which limits full automation. Overall, the exposure is high but not close to total.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Real estate agents in Sweden show clear early-stage AI adoption signals, but not yet broad, occupation-wide standard use. Observable current uses include AI-assisted property marketing copy, image enhancement/staging, lead qualification/chatbots on brokerage websites, CRM automation, meeting-note/transcription tools, and some support in valuation/comparable analysis. Larger firms and digital-first brokerages are more likely to deploy these tools, while many smaller agencies still rely mainly on traditional workflows. Unlike highly AI-mature occupations, there is no strong sign that core brokerage work is being routinely AI-driven across the sector yet. So this fits early adoption: tools exist and are used by some employers, but mainstream penetration is still limited.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Property managers mainly have a cognitive, screen-based role: leasing, inspections documentation, maintenance planning, rent negotiation, financial calculations, contract drafting, and reviewing offers. These are tasks where AI can assist strongly with analysis, drafting, comparison, and administrative processing. However, the occupation still includes substantial interpersonal negotiation and on-site property oversight/inspections, so it is not near the highest exposure category. In Sweden, legal, contractual, and relationship-heavy elements plus established work routines and collective agreements may slow full AI adoption, but the core task mix remains predominantly digital/knowledge-based.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Property managers have clear task segments where AI is currently being used in practice, but not yet in a deeply standardized way across the occupation. Observable adoption signals as of early 2026 include LLM-assisted drafting of emails, tenant communications, meeting notes, lease/contract templates, maintenance-plan text, and summarization of inspection reports and vendor offers. Property management platforms in Sweden and the Nordics also increasingly bundle AI features for customer service, ticket triage, document search, and predictive maintenance/energy optimization, which touch this role indirectly. However, the core occupation still relies heavily on relationship handling, site inspections, negotiation, legal/financial judgment, and local context, and there is no strong signal of near-universal AI tooling adoption comparable to software or customer support. In Sweden, larger private property firms are likely experimenting or selectively using AI, while smaller firms and public/municipal landlords tend to adopt more cautiously. Overall this looks like early adoption with some real deployment, but not yet mainstream.

description: Växlar och iordningställer tåg på bangårdar samt säkerhetssynar och provbromsar dem. Sköter vagnupptagningen, rangeringen, vagnexpediering och godshantering. Utför enklare underhåll av fordon.
descriptionEnglish: Switches and arranges trains at rail yards and performs safety checks and tests. Manages vehicle pick-up, shunting, wagon dispatch, and cargo handling. Performs minor maintenance on vehicles.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: No forecast available.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: No forecast available.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Yard personnel work mainly physically at rail yards with switching, shunting, safety checks, test braking, cargo handling, and simpler maintenance of vehicles. This makes the occupation clearly hands-on and dependent on real-time situations, safety, and physical presence. At the same time, there are certain subtasks where AI may contribute indirectly, for example planning, scheduling, shunting optimization, documentation, and troubleshooting/maintenance support. However, the core work is not screen- or knowledge-based, so the overall AI exposure is low to medium.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For yard personnel, there are currently very few clear observable AI adoption signals in the Swedish labor market. The tasks are highly operational and safety-critical, with much physical work, regulated shunting, braking tests, coupling/decoupling, and simpler vehicle maintenance. There is some technology-related digitization in the railway sector, but it mainly involves traditional automation, planning systems, sensors, and decision support rather than generative AI or clearly AI-driven workflow automation in actual yard work. I therefore see only marginal pilot/indirect use signals, not broad or clear current adoption. Therefore, current adoption lies around 1–2, and I set it at 1.5.

descriptionEnglish: Drives locomotives, multiple units, commuter trains, subways, and shunting locomotives in passenger and freight traffic.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Employment Agency assesses that there will be little competition for jobs for train drivers over the coming year. This means that individuals with the necessary education or experience will have great opportunities for work, although the job market may vary depending on the specialization within the profession.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: In a two-year perspective, the Employment Agency estimates that job opportunities for train drivers will be substantial. Opportunities for employment in the profession increase if you hold relevant certifications, qualifications, or vocational certificates.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Train drivers primarily work in a physical, safety-critical, and real-time controlled environment where the core is operating trains/locomotives, monitoring signals, following traffic rules, and managing deviations. AI can likely support certain parts like scheduling, route optimization, monitoring, troubleshooting, and decision support, but the actual driving and operational responsibility still require human presence and attention. In Sweden, the implementation of such systems is also slowed by safety requirements, regulations, and collective agreements. Therefore, the exposure is moderate but clearly limited by the physical and regulated work environment.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For train drivers, there are currently very few observable signs of direct AI tool adoption in day-to-day work. Train operations are highly regulated, safety-critical, and heavily standardized, which limits deployment of generative AI or decision-support tools in the cab. As of early 2026, the clearest AI-related activity appears to be indirect and organizational rather than occupation-specific: maintenance, scheduling, traffic management, and predictive analytics may use AI in the wider rail system, but not as a mainstream tool for drivers themselves. I therefore rate current adoption as minimal (1/10), reflecting at most scattered pilots or back-office use rather than meaningful deployment in the occupation.

scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Vehicle assemblers (SSYK 8211) mainly work with physical, repetitive assembly in mass production. Therefore, the current adoption is assessed as marginal, with some pilot or support systems, rather than mainstream use.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Operations technicians at heating and water plants combine continuous monitoring, control-room decisions, troubleshooting, logging, and compliance tasks with hands-on oversight of industrial plant equipment. Job ads and employer communications are more likely to mention automation, remote operation, and maintenance systems than explicit generative AI use. Where AI is present, it is usually embedded in industrial software or used by engineering/maintenance teams rather than as a directly visible frontline tool across the occupation. Styr och övervakar maskiner som tappar på flaskor, försluter, emballerar, packar, väger samt märker och fäster fraktsedlar m.m. på produkter, förpackningar och behållare.
 
Hit hör inte
Handpaketerare (9320)
Varumärkare (9320)
descriptionEnglish: Controls and monitors machines that fill bottles, seal, package, weigh, label, and attach shipping documents to products, packaging, and containers.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Employment Agency assesses that there will be little competition for jobs for machine operators in manufacturing over the coming year. This means that individuals with the necessary education or experience will have great opportunities for work, although the job market may vary depending on the specialization within the profession.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: In a two-year perspective, the Employment Agency estimates that job opportunities for machine operators in manufacturing will be substantial. Opportunities for employment in the profession increase if you hold relevant certifications, qualifications, or vocational certificates.
forecast.ingress: Bedömningen för maskinoperatörer inom tillverkning är en samlad bedömning för maskinoperatörer, cement-, sten- och betongvaror, maskinoperatörer, kött- och fiskberedningsindustri, maskinoperatörer, plastindustri, operatörer inom sågverk, hyvleri och plywood m.m., maskinoperatörer, kemisktekniska och fotografiska produkter, maskinoperatörer, pappersvaruindustri, övriga maskinoperatörer, textil-, skinn- och läderindustri, maskinoperatörer, mejeri, övriga maskinoperatörer, livsmedelsindustri m.m., maskinoperatörer, gummiindustri, maskinoperatörer, kvarn-, bageri- och konfektyrindustri, maskinoperatörer, ytbehandling, valsverksoperatörer, maskinställare och maskinoperatörer, metallarbete, maskinsnickare och maskinoperatörer, träindustri, maskinoperatörer inom ytbehandling, trä, maskinoperatörer, blekning, färgning och tvättning, maskinoperatörer, påfyllning, packning och märkning, manuella ytbehandlare, trä, maskinoperatörer, farmaceutiska produkter. Det finns cirka 115000 yrkesverksamma maskinoperatörer inom tillverkning i Sverige, varav cirka 21 procent är kvinnor och 79 procent är män.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: The occupation primarily involves controlling and monitoring packaging and labeling machines as well as handling refilling, packing, weighing, and freight notes in a physical production environment. There is some digital/cognitive component in monitoring, error indication, and production control that AI can support, but the core remains machine handling and practical work close to equipment. Therefore, the AI exposure is moderate to low, rather than high.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For this occupation, current AI adoption signals appear to be in the early-adoption range rather than mainstream. In Swedish packaging/filling/labeling machine operations, the core work is physically anchored on production lines, where automation and PLC/vision systems are common, but generative AI is not yet a standard daily tool. Observable AI use is mainly indirect: machine vision for inspection/label verification, predictive maintenance, anomaly detection, and some AI-supported production planning or quality monitoring in larger factories. These tools can affect the job, but they are typically embedded in equipment or backend systems rather than used directly by operators. There are some employers experimenting with smarter line monitoring and digital assistants, yet I do not see strong evidence of broad, routine AI use across this SSYK group in Sweden as of early 2026. So the occupation is best rated as early adoption with limited but real deployment.

description: Utför annat process- och maskinoperatörsarbete. Exempel på arbetsuppgifter: Styr och övervakar ugnar och andra anläggningar för tillverkning av glasmassa, glasprodukter, linser samt keramiska produkter som t.ex. kakel, keramik och tegel.
descriptionEnglish: Performs other work as a process and machine operator. Examples of tasks include controlling and monitoring furnaces and other facilities for producing glass mass, glass products, lenses, as well as ceramics such as tiles, pottery, and bricks.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: No forecast available.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: No forecast available.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: The work primarily involves controlling and monitoring furnaces and process facilities in the industrial production of glass, ceramics, and similar products. There are some digital and monitoring elements where AI can assist with alarm analysis, process optimization, and quality control, but the core of the occupation is strongly tied to physical facilities, machine monitoring, and practical process work. Therefore, the theoretical AI exposure is low to moderate, but clearly below a fully digital/knowledge-based job.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For SSYK 8189 (other process and machine operators in glass/ceramics/furnace and facility monitoring), as of early 2026, there are clear but still quite limited AI signals. There is some use of AI/ML in industrial monitoring, predictive maintenance, quality control via vision systems, and process optimization, but this is most often embedded in automation and control systems rather than as direct AI tools that operators use daily. For the operator role itself, it is therefore primarily pilot projects, vendor solutions, and some larger industry companies testing or partially using such tools, not broad and established adoption within the profession. Overall: marginal to early adoption, but not yet mainstream.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Retail salespersons in specialty retail have a distinctly mixed workday. Part of the work is cognitive and digital: informing about products and prices, providing advice, handling orders, price tagging, and cashier work, where AI can assist with product recommendations, stock and price information, and customer service. At the same time, a lot of physical presence in the store, practical goods management, customer meetings, and situationally adapted sales requiring human interaction are included. Overall, the exposure is moderate rather than high.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Retail salespersons in specialty retail have clear but still limited current adoption signals. There is already widespread use of AI in adjacent retail flows such as customer service chats, product recommendations, inventory/demand forecasting, campaign optimization, and simpler sales support in store systems. In Swedish retail, there are also increasing mentions of AI in job ads and tools for personalization and planning. However, the core work for retail salespersons remains very people-oriented: customer meetings, product demonstrations, needs analysis, and physical handling in the store. This means that AI is most often used as support for back office and sales support rather than being a direct replacement in the role. Overall, this points to early adoption with some real uses, but not broad or deeply integrated AI use in the profession's main tasks.

So this fits early adoption rather than significant adoption.

AI may assist with planning, documentation, fault analysis, and monitoring, but it cannot replace the hands-on work and safety-critical field operations. Overall, there is a low theoretical exposure to AI.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For distributionselektriker there are some observable AI signals, but they are still marginal in day-to-day work as of early 2026. The occupation is highly field-based and safety-critical, centered on physical inspection, fault repair, maintenance, and switching work on lines, substations, and transformers. Current AI use is mostly indirect: asset-management systems, sensor analytics, predictive maintenance, drone/imagery inspection, and decision-support tools used by grid operators and larger utilities. These tools can help prioritize faults or inspections, but they do not yet represent mainstream, routine AI use by distribution electricians themselves. Job ads and employer signals are more likely to mention digital monitoring, remote diagnostics, and data-driven maintenance than explicit AI tooling. Overall this fits a low-but-real adoption level rather than broad deployment.

scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For motor vehicle mechanics and vehicle repairers, current AI adoption signals are mostly marginal rather than mainstream. However, these are typically embedded features in specialized equipment rather than broadly adopted stand-alone AI workflows in everyday workshops. In Sweden, most of the occupation still relies on manual inspection, OBD diagnostics, service manuals, and technician experience. Job ads and employer signals more often emphasize digital diagnostics and EV competence than explicit AI use. Overall this looks like early, uneven adoption with some pilots and niche deployment, but not significant current penetration.

nameEnglish: Machine Setters and Machine Operators, Metalworking
description:
Sköter, styr och övervakar hel- och halvautomatiska maskiner som bearbetar arbetsstycken av metall eller liknande material genom borrning, hyvling, svarvning, fräsning, gängning, slipning, svetsning m.m. Programmerar datorstyrda maskiner och korrigerar program. Sköter maskiner som bearbetar och skär material med hjälp av elektroner. Hanterar maskiner som formar detaljer i metall genom pressning eller tryck samt maskiner som kapar eller klipper metallprodukter. Sätter upp, utprovar och besiktigar verktyg. Ställer in och programmerar hel- och halvautomatiska verktygsmaskiner.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: The profession is clearly mixed. A large part of the work is practical and physical: setting up, monitoring, changing tools, testing equipment, and handling machines in a workshop environment. At the same time, there is a significant digital/cognitive aspect in programming CNC-controlled machines, correcting programs, reading blueprints, and optimizing processes. AI can therefore assist in programming, troubleshooting, planning, and quality control, but it cannot replace the ongoing machine handling and the hands-on responsibility that is required. Overall, there is a medium level of exposure.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For SSYK 7223, there are currently some clear AI/automation-signalized applications, but they are not widespread enough to be considered mainstream. In metal processing, CNC/PLC-near software, machine monitoring, vision/quality control, and sometimes AI-based process optimization or predictive maintenance are used in larger workshops and industrial companies. There are also supplier tools that support programming, setup, and troubleshooting of machines. At the same time, practical adoption is often more about general automation/digitalization than actual AI, and many smaller Swedish workshops have limited implementation. Overall, observable signals point to early adoption: some employers are using or testing AI-related tools, but it is not yet a broad standard feature in the occupational role.

scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For carpenters/cabinetmakers (SSYK 7111), at the beginning of 2026, there are few clear, broad, and observable signals regarding actual AI use in daily work. The profession is heavily physical, location-based, and traditionally standardized through tools, drawings, measurements, and construction processes rather than digital decision support. Indeed, there are some adjacent AI signals in the construction sector, such as in planning, estimating, drawing review, quantity takeoff, documentation, and machine control, but these are more often found in management, design, or larger contractors rather than within the profession itself. Overall, this indicates marginal adoption, not mainstream.

OCCUPATION (SSYK code: 7119): Övriga byggnads- och anläggningsarbetare / Övriga byggnads- och anläggningsarbetare
DESCRIPTION: Utför annat bygg- och anläggningsarbete än inom 7111-7116. Exempel på arbetsuppgifter: Lägger ner och sammanfogar rör av betong, metall eller plast. Utför olika arbetsuppgifter i samband med husbyggen och byggreparationer, t.ex. byggnadsträ- och betongarbete. Monterar fabrikstillverkade betongelement.

Hit hör inte
Anläggningsarbetare (7114)
Träarbetare/Snickare (7111)


SCORING RUBRIC for "theoreticalExposure" (0–10):
- 9–10: Almost entirely screen/knowledge-based; output is text, code, data, or decisions AI models excel at
- 7–8: Majority of tasks are cognitive/digital but with some physical or interpersonal components
- 5–6: Mixed — significant cognitive tasks that AI could assist with, significant physical/relational tasks it cannot
- 3–4: Mostly physical, hands-on, interpersonal, or highly context-dependent (Swedish welfare/care setting)
- 1–2: Almost entirely physical, outdoor, or dependent on real-world embodiment
- 0: No plausible AI impact on core task structure

IMPORTANT CONTEXT (Sweden-specific):
- Sweden has high union density and strong collective agreements that slow adoption even in exposed occupations
- Many public sector roles (nurses, teachers, social workers) involve statutory duties that cannot be delegated to AI by law
- Consider the TASK composition, not just the job title
- High exposure does NOT mean job loss — demand may rise

description: Bedriver havs-, kust- och insjöfiske. Underhåller båtar och redskap.
descriptionEnglish: Engages in sea, coastal and freshwater fishing. Maintains boats and equipment.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: No forecast available.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: No forecast available.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Fishermen primarily work physically and outdoors with sea, coastal, and freshwater fishing as well as maintenance of boats and equipment. The core tasks require practical handling, real-time weather and situational assessment, and physical presence, which results in very low theoretical exposure to AI. AI may possibly support planning, route selection, weather analysis, or logistics, but does not significantly affect the structure of core work.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For Swedish fishers (marine, coastal, and freshwater fishing), there are very weak observable signals of direct AI adoption in day-to-day work as of early 2026. The occupation is dominated by manual vessel operation, gear handling, weather/sea-condition judgment, and maintenance—tasks where AI tools are not commonly embedded in workflows. There may be some indirect technology use in the broader sector (e.g., electronic logbooks, route planning, sonar/fish-finding equipment, weather optimization, and some fleet management software), but these are not strong, widespread AI adoption signals specific to the occupation. In Sweden, AI mentions and workplace adoption are rising, but for this occupation, the current penetration appears marginal and limited to isolated pilots or vendor features rather than mainstream use.

description: Odlar och inplanterar fisk och skaldjur.
descriptionEnglish: Cultivates and stocks fish and shellfish.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: No forecast available.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: No forecast available.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Fish farmers primarily work practically and physically with the cultivation, management, and stocking of fish and shellfish in real facilities. The core tasks depend on environmental conditions, animal handling, and ongoing on-site observation rather than digital or text-based work. AI may potentially support planning, monitoring, and analysis of sensor data, but the central work structure is still almost entirely hands-on and outdoor-based.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For Swedish fish farmers (fiskodlare) there are some observable AI-related signals, but they are still marginal in day-to-day operations. Aquaculture does use digital monitoring systems, sensor data, camera-based fish counting, feeding optimization, and occasional computer-vision/analytics pilots for health, growth, and environmental conditions. However, as of early 2026 there is little evidence that AI is broadly or routinely deployed across Swedish fish farms in the same way it is in office-based professions. Most adoption appears to be vendor-driven pilots or selective use of decision-support tools rather than mainstream workplace practice. So the current adoption level is best rated as early/marginal rather than moderate.

descriptionEnglish: Conducts forestry operations. Manages and fells forests.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Public Employment Service assesses that there will be competition for jobs as forestry workers over the next year. This means that individuals with the required education or experience will have small to medium opportunities for employment, although the labor market may vary depending on the specific focus within the profession.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: In the next two years, the Public Employment Service estimates that job opportunities for forestry workers will be small to medium. Employment prospects in the field increase if you have relevant certificates, qualifications, or professional credentials.
forecast.ingress: Det finns cirka 5600 yrkesverksamma skogsarbetare i Sverige, varav cirka 19 procent är kvinnor och 81 procent är män.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Forest workers mainly work practically in a forest environment, focusing on the care and harvesting of timber. The core of the work is physical, machine-based, and location-dependent rather than digital or text/computer-based. AI may potentially support planning, route optimization, inventory, or machine control, but the central tasks remain hands-on and are dependent on real-world conditions. Therefore, the exposure is assessed as low.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For skogsarbetare (forest workers), there are currently very few observable signs of direct AI adoption in routine field work as of early 2026. Core tasks like clearing, planting, thinning, and harvesting are still dominated by mechanized equipment, manual decisions, GPS/production systems, and sensor-based forestry tech rather than AI-native workflows. Some forestry companies may use AI indirectly in planning, remote sensing, satellite/drone analysis, or machine maintenance optimization, but these signals are mostly upstream and not meaningfully embedded in the day-to-day occupation itself. So current adoption is best rated as very low, near no meaningful direct deployment.

nameEnglish: Plant Growers and Animal Breeders, Mixed Farming
description: Bedriver växtodling kombinerat med avel och uppfödning av lantbrukets husdjur eller sällskapsdjur. Odlar och skördar jordbruksväxter samt sköter djur för att använda som lantbrukets husdjur för produktion av mjölk, kött, ägg, pälsskinn m.m. eller som sällskapsdjur.
descriptionEnglish: Engages in crop production combined with the breeding and raising of farm animals or pets. Cultivates and harvests crops and cares for animals to use as farm animals for the production of milk, meat, eggs, fur, etc., or as pets.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Public Employment Service assesses that there will be competition for jobs as growers of garden and agricultural plants as well as mixed farming animal breeders over the next year. This means that individuals with the required education or experience will have small to medium opportunities for employment, although the labor market may vary depending on the specific focus within the profession.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: In the next two years, the Public Employment Service estimates that job opportunities for growers of garden and agricultural plants as well as mixed farming animal breeders will be small to medium. Employment prospects in the field increase if you have relevant certificates, qualifications, or professional credentials.
forecast.ingress: Bedömningen för odlare av trädgårds- och jordbruksväxter samt djuruppfödare blandad drift är en samlad bedömning för växtodlare och djuruppfödare, blandad drift, odlare av jordbruksväxter, frukt och bär, trädgårdsodlare. Det finns cirka 17700 yrkesverksamma odlare av trädgårds- och jordbruksväxter samt djuruppfödare blandad drift i Sverige, varav cirka 19 procent är kvinnor och 81 procent är män.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation is dominated by hands-on, outdoor, and animal- and crop-based work: planting, harvesting, feeding, breeding, cleaning, monitoring animal health, and responding to weather, season, and farm conditions. AI can help somewhat with planning, recordkeeping, yield forecasting, feed optimization, disease detection, and market/weather analysis, but these are support functions rather than the core task structure. Because the job requires physical presence, real-world judgment, and continuous interaction with plants and animals, theoretical AI exposure is low to moderate rather than high.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For SSYK 6130 (mixed crop and livestock farmers), there are currently very few strong observable AI adoption signals in Sweden. The work is highly physical, seasonal, and site-specific, with AI use mostly limited to adjacent precision-agriculture tools (sensor systems, forecasting, machine vision, herd monitoring) rather than direct day-to-day generative AI or broadly deployed AI workflows. Some larger farms may use AI-enabled dairy monitoring, crop scouting, or equipment automation, but these are not yet mainstream across the occupation. As of early 2026, the occupation shows at most isolated niche use rather than meaningful widespread adoption.

scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For Swedish crop, fruit and berry growers there are some observable AI signals, but they are still limited and not mainstream. However, for this SSYK group most work remains physical and operational, with adoption constrained by farm size, cost, fragmented ownership, and the fact that many growers rely on machinery automation rather than general AI workflows. scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Firefighters' core tasks are primarily physical, location-bound, and time-critical: extinguishing fires, rescue operations, damage limitation in emergencies, and handling hazardous materials. AI can assist with planning, risk analysis, training, and informational materials on fire safety, but cannot replace the actual on-site intervention. Therefore, the theoretical exposure to AI is low to moderate, mainly concerning administrative and preventive components.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For firefighters, there are very weak and indirect AI adoption signals in today's Swedish labor market. The profession is highly operational, safety-critical, and regulated, with a focus on physical rescue operations, situational awareness, and on-site management rather than digital knowledge production. Indeed, there are some possible support tools in adjacent areas of the sector, such as AI for alarm triage, resource planning, image analysis from drones/cameras, or training simulations, but these are not clearly established as standard tools for firefighters in Sweden by early 2026. Additionally, the public sector and emergency services tend to have a slower and more cautious adoption rate. Overall, the observable signals indicate minimal current adoption in the profession itself, leaning more towards pilot/experimental levels rather than widespread use.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Telemarketers primarily work with information gathering, argumentation, customer contact, and closing deals over the phone, thus mainly engaging in digital and language-based tasks that AI can support or partially automate. The tasks are relatively standardizable: making calls to leads, presenting offers, addressing objections, scheduling meetings, and recording results in systems. At the same time, the profession requires a certain level of human intuition, improvisation, and social influence, which slows down the potential for full automation. In the Swedish context, collective agreements and the employer's control over customer relationships can also limit rapid replacement, but the theoretical exposure to AI remains high.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Telemarketers have clear, yet still mostly early, AI adoption signals. Today, there are well-known tools for call coaching, lead scoring, automated dialer optimization, transcription, CRM summaries, and generative support for call scripts and follow-up emails. These are used in some sales organizations and customer service/contact center environments, including in Sweden, but in the case of telemarketing, it is still not a widely standardized practice across all companies. The role is relatively susceptible to AI support since many tasks are repetitive and language-intensive, but observable signals as of early 2026 indicate more toward experimentation, selective rollout, and productivity enhancements rather than full maturity. Therefore, the assessment falls within early adoption rather than mainstream.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Cashier staff primarily perform digital and routine information tasks: registering products, processing payments, managing tickets, exchanging currency, and sometimes responding to simple customer inquiries. These tasks are relatively close to what AI and automated systems can support or partially replace, especially in checkout systems, self-checkout, and digital ticket sales. At the same time, there is a clear physical and interpersonal aspect to customer interactions, handling of payment methods, real-time service, and sometimes local knowledge/exceptions management, which means the work is not entirely screen-based. Overall, the majority of the job content is cognitive and process-driven, but with important human and practical components, resulting in high but not maximal AI exposure.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Current AI adoption signals for cashier/ticketing/currency-exchange roles in Sweden are still early and uneven. In retail and ticketing, AI is visible mainly through self-checkout, computer vision loss-prevention, chatbots, demand forecasting, and some cashier-assist features in POS systems, but these mostly support the operation rather than directly transform the cashier role. A few employers may use AI for staffing, customer interaction, or fraud detection, and some ticketing/customer-service channels increasingly use AI assistants. However, for the core tasks of taking payment, selling tickets, and handling currency exchange, there is not broad evidence of AI being routinely used by frontline staff at scale as of early 2026. So this is above marginal because tools and pilots are real, but below mainstream adoption.

scores.currentAdoptionRationale: At the beginning of 2026, there are very few clear, observable signals of AI adoption for burial and crematory staff. forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that there will be little competition for jobs for property caretakers over the next year. forecast.mediumTermOutlook: In two years, the Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that job opportunities for property caretakers will be great. Job opportunities in the profession increase if you have relevant certificates, qualifications, or professional credentials.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Property caretakers primarily work practically and on-site, handling supervision, maintenance, minor repairs, and service to tenants. scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For property managers, there are some observable AI signals, but they are still marginal in day-to-day work as of early 2026. Property management platforms and building operations systems increasingly include AI features for predictive maintenance, anomaly detection in HVAC/energy use, and smarter tenant/service ticket routing. Larger real estate owners and facility management firms in Sweden are starting to pilot these tools, especially for energy optimization and remote monitoring. However, the core occupation remains heavily manual and on-site, and most employers still rely on conventional maintenance systems rather than AI-driven workflows. So the current adoption level is best described as early adoption/limited experimentation rather than mainstream use.

descriptionEnglish: Carries out other beauty and body therapy work not covered by 5141-5144. Example duties include providing care treatments for hands, nails, and cuticles. Builds, reinforces, extends, and repairs nails using various methods, materials, and aids. Performs spa treatments. Advises on clothing and makeup.

forecast.mediumTermOutlook: No forecast available.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: The profession is primarily practical and customer-oriented: caring treatments for hands/nails, nail extensions, spa treatments, makeup, and masking are carried out physically on-site. There are certain knowledge and advisory components, such as providing advice on clothing and makeup as well as planning treatments, where AI can support with inspiration, booking, and product/style tips. However, the core work requires skill, precision, real-time aesthetic assessment, and direct customer interaction, resulting in a low to medium overall exposure to AI. Therefore, the profession is placed in the range of 3-4.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For this occupation, there are limited observable signals of meaningful AI deployment in day-to-day work as of early 2026. The core tasks are hands-on, physical, and client-facing (nail care, spa treatments, makeup, styling), which are not areas where AI is currently embedded in the actual service delivery. Some adjacent tools may be used in practice—such as AI-assisted booking, marketing content generation, social media planning, client consultation imagery, or virtual makeup/looks in consumer apps—but these are peripheral and not strong occupation-specific adoption signals. In Swedish workplace terms, this looks marginal: a few salons or freelancers may experiment with AI for marketing, scheduling, or inspiration, but there is little evidence of mainstream employer-driven AI tooling directly supporting the occupation itself.

descriptionEnglish: Cooks, plates, and garnishes food in the hotel and restaurant industry, in large kitchens, test kitchens, on a vessel, or in a shop.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Employment Agency assesses that there will be competition for jobs for chefs, head cooks, and sous chefs over the coming year. This means that individuals with the required education or experience will have limited to moderate opportunities for work, although the labor market may vary depending on the specific focus within the profession.

forecast.ingress: Bedömningen för kockar, köksmästare och souschefer är en samlad bedömning för köksmästare och souschefer, kockar och kallskänkor. Det finns cirka 42400 yrkesverksamma kockar, köksmästare och souschefer i Sverige, varav cirka 50 procent är kvinnor och 50 procent är män.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Chefs and cold kitchen staff have a distinctly mixed work profile. Part of the work is cognitive and can be supported by AI, such as menu planning, recipe optimization, purchasing, inventory management, nutritional calculations, scheduling and cost planning, as well as quality assurance through digital systems. At the same time, the core of the profession is strongly physical and situation-dependent: cooking, plating, garnishing, pace in the kitchen, hygiene, teamwork, and adaptation to ingredients and customer flows. In the Swedish context, the rapid automation of work is additionally limited by environmental requirements, collective agreements, and the fact that much of the work requires physical presence. Overall, this results in a medium level of AI exposure, but it is not primarily a digital profession.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For chefs and cold kitchen workers (SSYK 5120), there are some observable AI-related adoption signals in 2025–early 2026, but they are still marginal rather than mainstream. In Sweden, a few restaurants, hotel chains, catering firms, and food service operators are experimenting with AI for menu planning, demand forecasting, inventory/waste reduction, recipe scaling, and staffing/scheduling, and some vendors of POS, ordering, and kitchen-management systems now market AI features. However, the core work of preparing, plating, and garnishing food remains largely manual, and there is no evidence of widespread direct AI use by kitchen staff comparable to software, customer service, or analytics occupations. Overall this looks like early experimentation with limited deployment across the occupation, so a 3/10 fits best.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Guides and tour leaders primarily work on-site, managing groups, interacting with customers, and handling real-time situations during excursions and sightseeing tours. Overall, there are clear but still early signs of adoption, leaning more towards pilot/selective use than mainstream.

description: Svarar för passagerares säkerhet ombord på flygplanet. Informerar om säkerhetsföreskrifter m.m. Serverar måltider och drycker samt säljer olika varor. Planerar och samordnar service och aktiviteter på fartyg.
descriptionEnglish: Ensures the safety of passengers on board the aircraft. Informs about safety regulations. Serves meals and drinks, and sells various goods. Plans and coordinates service and activities on vessels.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: No forecast available.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: No forecast available.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Cabin crew primarily work with onboard safety, passenger service, and practical coordination in a physical, situational environment. Some aspects are governed by information and routines and can be partially supported by AI, for example, safety information, service planning, sales support, and staffing/logistics planning. However, the core tasks require presence, human interaction, real-time judgment, and handling of passengers onboard, which limits AI exposure. Overall, it is a mixed role but more physical and interpersonal than digital.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For Kabinpersonal m.fl. (cabin crew / onboard service staff), there are clear AI-enabling tools in the market today, but observable adoption is still mostly early-stage rather than mainstream. Airlines and ferry operators use AI indirectly in areas like scheduling, disruption handling, customer messaging, translation, training/simulation, and some service automation, but the core job tasks—safety briefings, onboard supervision, passenger service, and sales—still rely heavily on human execution and regulatory procedures. In Swedish and Nordic contexts, there are some visible experiments and digitalization projects, yet limited evidence that AI is broadly embedded in day-to-day cabin crew workflows across employers. That makes current adoption best fit as early adoption, not significant widespread use.

scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For mail carriers and postal terminal workers, there are currently few clear, broad signals of AI adoption. There is some use of automation, image interpretation, and planning systems in postal and terminal environments, but as of early 2026, AI appears to be primarily experimental or embedded in larger operating systems rather than something many employees actively use on a daily basis. Therefore, the assessment falls on the margin of early adoption.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: The occupation is largely knowledge- and office-based: listing, sorting, cataloging, registering, information retrieval, and lending of materials are tasks where AI can already support or partially automate work processes. At the same time, there are certain physical and practical elements, such as handling archival documents, books, museum objects, and managing audiovisual equipment, which means that the job is not entirely digital. Overall, the theoretical exposure to AI is quite high but not maximal.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Current adoption signals for Arkiv- och biblioteksassistenter m.fl. are best characterized as early adoption rather than mainstream use. There are observable AI tools relevant to parts of the work—especially search, metadata assistance, OCR/document extraction, transcription, catalog support, and basic recommendation/search functions in library and archive systems—but these are typically embedded in broader information systems rather than deployed as clearly AI-led workflows. In Swedish public-sector and cultural-institution settings, adoption is generally slower, with many workplaces still using traditional processes and only selective experimentation or limited rollouts. The occupation also includes hands-on tasks (handling physical collections, lending, exhibition support, audiovisual equipment) that are not strongly AI-driven. Overall, there are meaningful current adoption signals, but they are uneven and not yet mainstream across employers, so a mid-low early-adoption score is appropriate.

descriptionEnglish: Coordinates and supervises passenger and freight transport, for instance, at local transport companies, freight forwarders, cargo terminals, wholesales, and industrial companies. Develops and adjusts transport schedules.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Employment Agency assesses that there will be little competition for jobs for transport managers and transport coordinators over the coming year. This means that individuals with the required education or experience will have good opportunities for work, although the labor market may vary depending on the specific focus within the profession.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: In the next two years, the Employment Agency assesses that the opportunities for work for transport managers and transport coordinators will be good. Job prospects in the field increase if you have relevant certifications, qualifications, or vocational credentials.
forecast.ingress: Det finns cirka 8900 yrkesverksamma transportledare och transportsamordnare i Sverige, varav cirka 30 procent är kvinnor och 70 procent är män.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: The work is clearly information- and coordination-intensive: planning and adjusting transport schedules, monitoring traffic, calculating arrival times, recording departures/landings, and making ongoing operational decisions. These tasks are quite exposed to AI since systems can support optimization, forecasting, monitoring, and routine documentation. At the same time, the profession involves important real-time monitoring, safety responsibilities, coordination with other stakeholders, and sometimes physical presence in traffic environments (trains, airports, terminals, docks), which suggests that AI is likely to be primarily a decision support tool rather than a complete replacement. Overall: medium to relatively high theoretical exposure.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: There are clear but still limited current AI signals for this profession. Transport management and transport coordination are already using digital planning and optimization systems, and some employers in logistics, terminal, and traffic environments are starting to add AI for route optimization, forecasting, staffing planning, exception management, and automated monitoring. At the same time, most of the core role still involves rule-based operational traffic management, safety-critical supervision, and real-time coordination where classic TMS/WMS/SCADA-like systems dominate and human responsibility remains. In Sweden, therefore, early adoption and selective use are more prominent rather than broad, mainstream AI utilization in the profession as a whole. This suggests a position around the middle of the 4-scale, not 6+.

descriptionEnglish: Records, controls, and manages material and goods flows. Plans and controls deliveries and oversees storage and utilization of warehouse capacity.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Employment Agency assesses that there will be competition for jobs for warehouse and terminal staff over the coming year. This means that individuals with the required education or experience will have limited to moderate opportunities for work, although the labor market may vary depending on the specific focus within the profession.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: In the next two years, the Employment Agency assesses that the opportunities for work for warehouse and terminal staff will be limited to moderate. Job prospects in the field increase if you have relevant certifications, qualifications, or vocational credentials.
forecast.ingress: Det finns cirka 84300 yrkesverksamma lager- och terminalpersonal i Sverige, varav cirka 22 procent är kvinnor och 78 procent är män.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Warehouse and terminal staff have a mixture of digital and physical tasks. The cognitive aspect is clear: registering and controlling flows, planning deliveries and warehouse utilization, as well as managing documentation and systems. These activities are relatively exposed to AI, particularly regarding planning, deviation control, and administrative decision support. At the same time, there is a lot of practical work in warehouses/terminals, such as receiving goods, picking, packing, and loading with forklifts, which requires physical presence and real-time assessment. Overall, the exposure is medium rather than high, as AI can support large parts of the information and planning work but cannot replace the physical handling.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale:
For warehouse and terminal staff, there are clear but still primarily early/ad hoc signals of AI in today’s workplace. What is broadly visible is not that the actual forklift, picking, or loading moments are AI-driven, but rather that AI/support tools are used around them: forecasts for inventory management, order prioritization, demand planning, exception handling, route/dock planning, and simpler administrative tasks in WMS/ERP environments. In Swedish job advertisements and businesses, there are increasing demands for digital inventory and logistics systems, and larger players in e-commerce, third-party logistics, and industry are testing AI for inventory optimization and staffing.

At the same time, there are few clear signs that AI is mainstream in the daily operational core for the majority of these jobs. Many tasks are still physical, standardized, and controlled by scanners, warehouse computers, and fixed processes rather than generative AI. Therefore, the adoption is considered early but real—not low or nonexistent, and not at the level of professions where AI is already a common tool.

forecast.mediumTermOutlook: No forecast available.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: The role of a work leader in warehousing and terminals involves a mix of digital and physical tasks. The position entails planning, scheduling, coordination, reporting, monitoring of flows, and sometimes decision support regarding staffing and logistics, which AI can assist with very effectively. At the same time, the job requires on-site presence, ongoing contact with staff, handling of deviations, safety, and practical management of daily operations in a physical environment. Overall, the exposure is clear but not dominant, which suits a medium level.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For arbetsledare inom lager och terminal finns clear AI-relevant tooling, but current observable adoption looks mostly early-stage rather than mainstream. In Sweden, warehouses and terminals are increasingly using WMS/TMS systems with forecasting, route/slot optimization, exception handling, and simple copilots for planning, reporting, and communication, but these are usually embedded in logistics software rather than standalone AI workflows owned by the supervisor role itself. Job ads and vendor cases suggest some experimentation with AI for demand planning, staffing, quality control, and incident reporting, yet there is not strong evidence that most Swedish warehouse/terminal supervisors regularly use AI day-to-day in the way that office-based knowledge roles do. Overall: some real deployment signals, but still patchy and not yet standard across employers.

description: Intervjuar personer och hanterar svaren för marknads-, samhälls- och opinionsundersökningar.
descriptionEnglish: Interviews individuals and processes responses for market, social, and opinion surveys.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: No forecast available.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: No forecast available.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Market researchers and interviewers primarily work with asking questions, recording answers, compiling data, and sometimes conducting preliminary analysis of survey results. These are mainly cognitive and digital tasks that AI can support through automated interview administration, transcription, coding of responses, quality control, and preliminary analysis. At the same time, there are certain human elements involved in contacting respondents, motivating participation, and managing interview situations, which means that the tasks are not entirely automatable. Overall, the profession is clearly exposed to AI but not completely so.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For market researchers and interviewers, there are clear but still limited current adoption signals. AI is already being used for parts of the job such as script suggestions, form/survey drafts, transcription, coding of open responses, quality control, and simpler compilations. There are also observable tools in survey platforms and call center/research environments that automate or assist parts of data collection and analysis. At the same time, the core task itself—conducting interviews, following up on responses, and managing respondent contact—is still heavily reliant on human interaction, which means that full-scale AI dominance is not yet mainstream. In Sweden, early to moderate adoption is therefore primarily visible: some employers and agencies use AI in production, but for many it is still more of a pilot, support tool, or selective use rather than a standard across the profession.

description: Tar emot besökande. Informerar och hänvisar till plats, lokal eller person. Hanterar in- och utpassering.
descriptionEnglish: Welcomes visitors. Provides information and refers them to places, rooms, or persons. Manages access control.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Employment Agency assesses that there will be competition for jobs for office receptionists over the coming year. This means that individuals with the required education or experience will have limited to moderate opportunities for work, although the labor market may vary depending on the specific focus within the profession.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: In the next two years, the Employment Agency assesses that the opportunities for work for office receptionists will be limited to moderate. Job prospects in the field increase if you have relevant certifications, qualifications, or vocational credentials.
forecast.ingress: Det finns cirka 12100 yrkesverksamma kontorsreceptionister i Sverige, varav cirka 86 procent är kvinnor och 14 procent är män.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Office receptionists have a mixed workload. Some of the tasks are information and communication-based, such as welcoming visitors, providing directions, and managing entry and exit, which AI can support through digital reception services, booking systems, chatbots, and automated visitor registration. At the same time, the role often requires physical presence, face-to-face service, situational assessment, and management of practical deviations in real time. Therefore, the exposure is clear but not high enough for a rating of 7–8.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Receptionists have clear but still mostly early adoption signs. There are already widely available AI supports in reception and visitor flows, such as chatbots for visitor support, automated responses to frequently asked questions, smart booking and referral systems, speech-to-text/translation, and camera/access control with AI features. In Swedish organizations, there is also increasing use of AI in customer contact and internal services, which allows parts of the reception's information and referral work to be alleviated. At the same time, it is not a mature, standardized professional tool at the same level as, for example, in development or customer service, and physical presence, security routines, and human hospitality/assessment mean that many workplaces still use AI selectively or test on a small scale. Overall: clear early adoption signals, but not yet mainstream across the professional field.

description: Tar emot, checkar in och checkar ut gäster på hotell, konferensanläggning, vandrarhem eller campingplats m.m. Informerar om bl.a. anläggningens service samt sevärdheter och nöjesevenemang. Gör i ordning konferenslokaler och ansvarar för att den tekniska utrustningen fungerar och att förtäring serveras enligt beställningen.
descriptionEnglish: Welcomes, checks in, and checks out guests in hotels, conference facilities, hostels, or campsites. Informs about the facility’s services and attractions.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Employment Agency assesses that there will be competition for jobs for hotel receptionists over the coming year. This means that individuals with the required education or experience will have limited to moderate opportunities for work, although the labor market may vary depending on the specific focus within the profession.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: In the next two years, the Employment Agency assesses that the opportunities for work for hotel receptionists will be limited to moderate. Job prospects in the field increase if you have relevant certifications, qualifications, or vocational credentials.
forecast.ingress: Det finns cirka 8700 yrkesverksamma hotellreceptionister i Sverige, varav cirka 73 procent är kvinnor och 27 procent är män.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Hotel receptionists work in a blend of digital and personal elements. A large part of the job is screen- and information-based: check-ins and check-outs, bookings, answering questions, referrals to attractions and events, as well as certain administration related to conferences and orders. These tasks are relatively exposed to AI and can be partially automated or supported by AI. At the same time, there is also a significant amount of customer contact, real-time problem solving, service, on-site coordination, and practical tasks such as arranging conference rooms and ensuring that technical equipment and catering function properly. This makes the profession clearly defined but not entirely digital, placing it in the middle of the scale.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For hotel receptionists in Sweden, there are clear current AI adoption signals, but they are still uneven and not yet deeply embedded across the occupation. Hotels and conference venues increasingly use AI-enabled front-desk and guest-service tools such as chatbots, automated booking and check-in flows, multilingual FAQ assistants, demand forecasting, and smart concierge features. These are observable in hospitality software offerings and in some employer implementations, especially larger chains and urban properties. However, the occupation itself still relies heavily on human-facing service, issue handling, local knowledge, and coordination of in-person operations, so AI is more of an assistive layer than a core workflow. In Sweden, the best evidence points to early adoption rather than mainstream saturation: some employers are experimenting and some tools are deployed, but broad standardized use across the role is not yet common. That places currentAdoption around 5.

description: Tar emot och kopplar telefonsamtal. Ger upplysningar, tar emot meddelanden och lämnar besked. Sköter växel samt hänvisnings- och personsökningssystem.
descriptionEnglish: Receives and connects phone calls. Provides information, takes messages, and delivers notifications. Manages switchboard and directory services.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: No forecast available.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: No forecast available.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Telephone operators mainly work with digital and linguistic information management: receiving and connecting calls, providing information, taking messages, and managing switchboard/referral systems. This is a clearly screen- and routine-driven type of task where AI can already automate large parts of call routing, FAQ responses, message capturing, and forwarding. At the same time, some human judgment is required for unclear cases, exceptions, prioritizations, and occasionally customer interactions, which means the role is not entirely automatable. In a Swedish context, collective agreements and organizational requirements may slow down implementation, but the task structure itself is highly exposed to AI.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Receptionists and call center-like communication services exhibit clear, observable signals of AI adoption today. Many Swedish organizations are already utilizing AI-based telephony systems, speech-to-text, call routing, chat/voice bots, and intelligent switchboards to alleviate or partially replace manual operators. This is particularly evident in customer service, larger private employers, healthcare emergency services, municipal contact centers, and standard telecommunications and IVR solutions offered by providers. At the same time, full replacement is uncommon, and human intervention is still needed for complex cases, language, prioritization, and exceptions. Overall, this is more than an early pilot phase but not fully automated, which warrants a strong yet not maximal adoption score.

description: Tar emot, samordnar och förmedlar larm om inträffade eller befarade olyckor i luften, på land och till sjöss. Leder arbetet med räddningsinsatser. Tar emot inbrottslarm, överfallslarm, driftlarm och trygghetslarm på larmcentral eller ledningscentral för bevakningsföretag.
descriptionEnglish: Receives, coordinates, and relays alarms about incidents or feared accidents in air, on land, and at sea. Leads work on rescue operations.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: No forecast available.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: No forecast available.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: SOS operators primarily work in digital management and alarm environments, receiving, interpreting, prioritizing, and forwarding alerts, as well as coordinating rescue efforts. This is a highly information- and decision-intensive job where AI can assist in filtering incoming cases, classification, prioritization, information retrieval, and suggestions for actions. At the same time, the role requires human judgment, responsibility in critical situations, communication with stressed individuals, and collaboration with emergency services, which means that the tasks are not entirely automatable. Overall: high but not maximal AI exposure.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For SOS operators/alarm center operators, there are certain clear AI signals, but they still resemble early pilot stages rather than widely implemented work methods. In practice, parts of AI/automation are already being used today in alarm environments, primarily for speech-to-text/transcription, call prioritization, triage support, pattern recognition in incoming alarms, and decision support in management and monitoring systems. At the same time, the core task is urgent, critical to safety, and heavily regulated, which means that the human operator must still take primary responsibility for assessment, coordination, and resource allocation. This means that adoption is more limited than in, for example, customer service or office work. In a Swedish context, it is reasonable to view this as "early adoption" with individual supplier and organizational implementations, but not yet widespread mainstream use among the majority of employers.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Customer service personnel primarily work with digital, text-based, and knowledge-based tasks: answering questions, giving advice, following policies, and managing standardized cases. This is precisely the type of language and decision-making work where AI can provide strong support through chatbots, automated responses, case classification, and knowledge support. However, the role still involves human interaction, exception handling, empathy, and accountability in more complex customer cases, which means that exposure is high but not maximal.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Customer service is one of the most prominent current use cases for AI in the workplace, and there are strong observable signs in Sweden and internationally: AI-supported chatbots, suggested responses, case prioritization, automated classification, and summarization of customer cases are already being used by many companies. For customer service staff, the adoption often does not involve full replacement but rather using AI as a work support tool in CRM/helpdesk systems. In the Swedish context, this is particularly evident among larger private employers, including telecom, e-commerce, banking/insurance, and outsourcing. At the same time, usage is not entirely mainstream across all sectors, and the public sector is slower, typically rated around 8-10. Overall, the profession is best placed in the 6-7 range, close to 7, as significant tooling is available and many employers are indeed using it in production.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Ship officers have a varied work profile. A significant portion of the job is cognitive and information-intensive: navigation, route planning, monitoring of position and weather, communication, documentation, and decision-making regarding safety and operations. These aspects have clear potential for AI support through decision-making aids, optimization, and monitoring systems. At the same time, the role requires continued physical presence on board, responsibility for the crew and the ship, real-time assessments in a dynamic environment, and compliance with safety regulations, which limits how much AI can replace the core work. Overall, the exposure is medium rather than high.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For Swedish ship officers (SSYK 3152), there are some observable AI-adjacent signals in 2025/early 2026, but they remain limited and non-mainstream. The occupation is heavily safety-regulated, operational, and tied to certified procedures, which constrains rapid AI deployment. Current use is more likely in supporting functions around navigation planning, route/weather optimization, maintenance prediction, documentation, and fleet operations rather than core watchkeeping or command decisions. Publicly visible evidence points to pilots and vendor offerings in maritime decision-support, autonomous navigation research, and sensor/fleet analytics, but not broad routine adoption by Swedish ship officers themselves. Compared with occupations where LLMs or copilots are already standard, this remains early adoption with some experimentation rather than significant tooling penetration.

The occupation’s core tasks — drafting letters and minutes, handling inquiries, registering/quality-checking information, routine calculations and reconciliations — map directly to widely deployed AI and automation tools: LLM-based drafting/summarization, email and ticket assistance, document classification, OCR/data extraction, meeting-note generation, and workflow automation in office suites and case-management systems. In Sweden, these tools are increasingly visible in job ads and workplace software, and many employers in both private and public sectors are beginning to use them for administrative support. However, adoption is still uneven and often partial: many workplaces use AI as an assistive layer rather than full workflow replacement, and public-sector and smaller employers remain more cautious. So the current state is best characterized as significant but not universal adoption, around the mid-high range rather than top-end.

scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Economic assistants have clear, observable AI adoption signals today, as their tasks are heavily text-, rule-, and transaction-based. At the same time, the adoption is not fully pervasive: many employers still utilize rule-based automation and standardized financial systems rather than advanced AI, and the implementation is slower in smaller companies and the public sector. In Swedish workplace terms, there is not yet strong evidence of broad employer adoption of AI as a standard tool in this occupation; usage appears limited to early experimentation by some venues, event firms, and production teams. descriptionEnglish: Responsible for technology and leads work during broadcasting of TV programs from studio or outside broadcasts.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Employment Agency assesses that there will be competition for jobs for audio, light, and image technicians over the coming year. This means that individuals with the required education or experience will have limited to moderate opportunities for work, although the labor market may vary depending on the specific focus within the profession.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: In the next two years, the Employment Agency assesses that the opportunities for work for audio, light, and image technicians will be good. Job prospects in the field increase if you have relevant certifications, qualifications, or vocational credentials.
forecast.ingress: Bedömningen för ljud, ljus- och bildtekniker är en samlad bedömning för ljus-, ljud- och scentekniker, bild- och sändningstekniker. Det finns cirka 4400 yrkesverksamma ljud, ljus- och bildtekniker i Sverige, varav cirka 23 procent är kvinnor och 77 procent är män.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: The profession is clearly mixed: a large part of the work is technical and digital, involving planning, equipment management, editing, mixing, and transferring audio/video between media where AI can provide strong support (e.g., automatic mixing, quality control, logging, and preparation). At the same time, live broadcasts, studio/OB bus work, and responsibility for real-time technical operations are included, which require physical presence, quick human judgment, and coordination that AI cannot easily replace. Overall, this results in a medium level of exposure.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale:
For Image and Broadcasting Technicians (SSYK 3521), there are clear current AI signals, but they appear to be in the early stages rather than mainstream. In broadcasting and studio production, AI is being utilized for tasks such as transcription, captioning, speech-to-text, rough video/audio tagging, media search, noise reduction, upscaling, and some editing assistance. These tools are increasingly integrated into production software and cloud services, meaning that the occupation is currently experiencing real adoption.

However, the core tasks of live broadcast operation, studio coordination, signal handling, mixing work, and technical responsibility during transmissions remain highly manual, real-time, and critical for safety and quality. This indicates that employers are more likely to use AI as an aid within the workflow rather than as a substitute for central job functions. In Sweden, the adoption of AI in media and broadcasting is likely uneven across different employers, with larger broadcasters and post-production companies taking the lead over smaller ones.

Overall: observable, practical AI use exists, but it has not yet transformed the occupation significantly. This reflects early adoption rather than broad mainstream implementation.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation is largely digital and knowledge-based: installing, configuring, troubleshooting, and maintaining network equipment such as firewalls, routers, and switches. AI can strongly assist with diagnostics, configuration generation, log analysis, documentation, monitoring, and routine maintenance planning. However, the role still involves on-site physical work, real-world infrastructure access, coordination with customers/colleagues, and responsibility for security-sensitive decisions, so it is not fully screen-based. In Sweden, adoption may be moderated by collective agreements and organizational practices, but the core task structure is still highly exposed to AI.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For Swedish network and systems technicians, there are clear but still limited current AI adoption signals as of early 2026. AI-assisted network monitoring, anomaly detection, log analysis, and ticket triage are commercially available and are being used by some larger employers and managed service providers. Network vendors and ITSM platforms also increasingly embed AI features for configuration help, incident summarization, and troubleshooting. However, for this occupation the core work remains hands-on infrastructure installation, maintenance, and administration, and there is not yet strong evidence that AI is broadly embedded across Swedish employers in the way it is for software development or customer support. Adoption appears uneven, with more use in larger private-sector IT operations than in smaller firms or public-sector environments. Overall this fits early adoption rather than mainstream deployment.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: IT support technicians perform mostly screen-based, knowledge-intensive work: troubleshooting, analyzing errors, guiding users, and documenting/handling installation and maintenance issues. These tasks are highly exposed to AI through diagnostic assistants, knowledge-base retrieval, scripted support workflows, and automated password/reset and ticket triage tools. The role still includes some human interaction, contextual judgment, and hands-on system support, so it is not at the very top of the scale, but the core task structure is strongly digital and AI-exposed.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Current AI adoption for Swedish IT support technicians is moderate and clearly observable, but not yet ubiquitous. Many organizations now use AI-enabled helpdesk/chatbots, ticket triage, knowledge-base search, and agent-assist tools in IT support workflows, and these are increasingly visible in enterprise software stacks. Swedish employers are also more likely than a few years ago to mention AI-assisted support or automation in job ads and internal tooling. However, the occupation still relies heavily on hands-on troubleshooting, access management, device/system administration, and user interaction, so AI is augmenting rather than replacing core work. Adoption is stronger in larger private-sector organizations and more limited in smaller firms and public sector environments. Overall this fits a solid early-to-mid adoption signal, around 6.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: The large kitchen manager has a clearly mixed set of responsibilities. There are observable signs of experimentation with digital decision support in public-sector and large private food service operations, but this occupation remains highly operational and hands-on, with adoption constrained by public procurement, legacy systems, and the need for practical kitchen execution. As of early 2026, this appears to be marginal-to-early adoption rather than significant mainstream use.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Chefs and sous chefs have a clear mix of cognitive and practical tasks. AI can assist with menu planning, recipe development, purchasing, scheduling, inventory management, and cost optimization—areas that are text- and decision-intensive. At the same time, the role requires daily leadership in the kitchen, quality control, coordination of staff, management of stressful operational situations, and often a physical presence in the kitchen environment. Since the core work is both operational and social, yet includes several administrative and analytical aspects that AI can support, the exposure is moderate.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For chefs and sous chefs, there are clear but still limited current adoption signals at the beginning of 2026. AI is already being used in some restaurants and food service environments for menu planning, recipe development, purchasing forecasts, waste optimization, staffing planning, and sometimes language support in administrative tasks. There is also broad access to generative AI tools that can assist with menu texts, allergen information, and standardized processes. At the same time, the daily core of the role is strongly practical, operational, and quality-driven in kitchen environments, which means that actual AI integration into the work itself is not yet mainstream. In Sweden, therefore, adoption seems to be at an early but genuine experimentation/selective implementation level rather than widespread, established usage.

scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For this occupation, there are clear current AI usage signals but not yet broad, standardized adoption. Unlike strongly AI-embedded professions, there is no evidence of near-universal workflow integration across employers, and adoption likely varies substantially by organization size and digital maturity.
