scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: System administrators primarily work to monitor, maintain, improve, and sometimes troubleshoot IT systems that are already operational. The core of the job is digital, knowledge-based, and documentation-intensive: analyzing logs, incident management, configuration, updates, process improvement, and coordination with users/providers. This closely aligns with what AI models excel at, particularly for monitoring, troubleshooting, routine documentation, and suggesting improvements. The role may have some human and organizational elements, but these do not alter the fact that the central work structure is heavily exposed to AI.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: System administrators have clear, observable AI adoption signals today, but not yet at the same level as pure developer roles. In Swedish organizations, AI tools are already being used for operational monitoring, troubleshooting, log analysis, incident triage, documentation, and for suggesting configuration or change actions. Many larger employers and providers have also integrated AI features into ITSM/observability and cloud platforms that system administrators actually use in their work. At the same time, usage is uneven, particularly in the public sector, where adoption is slower and many tasks are still handled traditionally. Overall, this indicates significant but not yet fully mainstream adoption, around 6.

description: Planerar, utvecklar, dokumenterar och genomför tester på olika nivåer, från enskilda programvaror till hela system. Verifierar att program och system fungerar enligt användarkrav, fastställda riktlinjer och regelverk.
descriptionEnglish: Plan, develop, document, and carry out tests at various levels, from individual software to entire systems. Verify that programs and systems operate according to user requirements, established guidelines, and regulations.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that there will be little competition for jobs for system testers and test managers in the coming year. This means that individuals with the required education or experience will have great opportunities for employment, but the job market may vary depending on the focus within the profession.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: Over a two-year period, the Swedish Public Employment Service estimates that job prospects for system testers and test managers will be significant. Employment opportunities in this field increase if you have relevant certifications, qualifications, or professional proof.
forecast.ingress: Det finns cirka 6900 yrkesverksamma systemtestare och testledare i Sverige, varav cirka 35 procent är kvinnor och 65 procent är män.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: System testers and test leaders work almost entirely with digital and knowledge-intensive tasks: test planning, test design, documentation, test execution, verification against requirements and regulations, as well as error analysis. Most of the work consists of text, data, logic, and decisions that AI can already assist significantly with, particularly in test case generation, log analysis, regression testing, and reporting. There are certain human elements involved, such as coordination with developers and stakeholders, as well as responsibility for quality assurance, but these do not change the fact that the core work is highly exposed. Swedish collective agreements may slow down the implementation, but they do not affect the technical exposure level of the tasks.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Systems testers and test leaders have clear current adoption signals in 2026, but penetration is not fully broad or uniform. In practice, AI is already being used for test case generation, requirements and documentation support, regression analysis, log/error pattern analysis, code-level test automation, and prioritization of test suites through Copilot/LLM-based tools, particularly in private tech and consulting firms. There are also several commercial testing tools with built-in AI, and many Swedish employers mention AI skills or AI support in testing and QA roles in job postings. At the same time, adoption remains uneven: much of the work is rule-based, quality-assured, and often tied to legacy environments, leading to AI primarily functioning as assistance rather than full automation. In the public sector and safety-critical operations, usage is still more cautious. Overall, this corresponds to significant but not universal adoption.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: System analysts and IT architects primarily work with digital and knowledge-intensive tasks: requirements analysis, system design, IT strategy, architectural principles, documentation, and decision support. These activities are very well suited for AI support, especially for analysis, proposals for architectural alternatives, code and documentation generation, as well as compiling requirements and risks. At the same time, the role often requires an understanding of business, balancing technology, security, cost, and organization, as well as collaboration with many stakeholders, which suggests that AI is likely to assist rather than fully replace the entire job. scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For SSYK 2511 (systemanalytiker och IT-arkitekter), there are strong current adoption signals in Sweden by early 2026. This occupation sits close to the software, cloud, data, and enterprise architecture functions where AI tools are already widely used: LLM-based coding assistants, architecture/documentation drafting, requirements analysis support, solution design brainstorming, and summarization of technical and business materials. Swedish employers increasingly mention AI literacy and AI-assisted development in IT/tech roles, and many organizations are actively piloting or using copilots and internal enterprise AI tools in these functions. However, adoption is not yet fully uniform across all employers, and in public sector and highly regulated environments it is still more cautious and uneven. So the right level is significant adoption, but not universal saturation.

nameEnglish: Market Analysts and Marketers and Others
description: Utvecklar, planerar och samordnar reklamkampanjer och marknadsföringsstrategier. Samlar in, bearbetar och analyserar data om marknader. Gör prognoser om framtida marknader och trender samt ger råd om försäljningsstrategier. Bedömer marknaden för nya varor och tjänster. Identifierar och utvecklar marknadsmöjligheter för nya och befintliga varor och tjänster.
descriptionEnglish: Develop, plan, and coordinate advertising campaigns and marketing strategies. Collect, process, and analyse data about markets. Make forecasts about future markets and trends and provide advice on sales strategies. Assess the market for new goods and services. Identify and develop marketing opportunities for new and existing goods and services.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that there will be competition for jobs for marketing analysts and marketers in the coming year. This means that individuals with the required education or experience will have small to medium employment opportunities, but the job market may vary depending on the focus within the profession.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: Over a two-year period, the Swedish Public Employment Service estimates that job prospects for marketing analysts and marketers will be small to medium. Employment opportunities in this field increase if you have relevant certifications, qualifications, or professional proof.
forecast.ingress: Det finns cirka 20400 yrkesverksamma marknadsanalytiker och marknadsförare i Sverige, varav cirka 54 procent är kvinnor och 46 procent är män.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: The profession is primarily knowledge- and screen-based: gathering, processing, and analyzing market data, forecasting, segmentation, campaign planning, and strategic recommendations. These are tasks where AI is already strong in compiling data, identifying patterns, generating forecasts, writing documentation, and suggesting marketing strategies. At the same time, there is a degree of human/contextual assessment, customer and internal collaboration, as well as responsibility for business decisions, which suggests that AI is likely to assist rather than fully replace the role. Overall, there is a high theoretical exposure.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Market analysts and marketers today have clear, observable AI adoption signals, particularly for content creation, campaign planning, segmentation, analysis, and forecasting. Tools like LLM-based copywriting, image/ad generation, social listening, CRM/marketing automation with built-in AI, and analytics support are already being used in many Swedish and international companies. There are also strong signals in job advertisements and workflows indicating that AI skills are in demand within marketing and market analysis. At the same time, the usage is not as deeply integrated as in pure tech roles; adoption varies significantly between larger private sectors and smaller companies/public sectors. Overall, this suggests significant but not complete maturity: around 7/10.

descriptionEnglish: Plan and organise recruitment, development, redeployment, and layoff of staff. Establish staff budgets and training plans. Participate in negotiations regarding salaries and employment terms and interpret and inform about labour law and agreements.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that there will be competition for jobs for personnel and HR specialists in the coming year. This means that individuals with the required education or experience will have small to medium employment opportunities, but the job market may vary depending on the focus within the profession.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: Over a two-year period, the Swedish Public Employment Service estimates that job prospects for personnel and HR specialists will be significant. Employment opportunities in this field increase if you have relevant certifications, qualifications, or professional proof.
forecast.ingress: Det finns cirka 21000 yrkesverksamma personal- och hr-specialister i Sverige, varav cirka 78 procent är kvinnor och 22 procent är män.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: The profession is primarily office- and knowledge-based: recruitment, personnel planning, training plans, salary and terms negotiations, as well as the interpretation of labor law regulations and agreements. Much of the work consists of gathering, analyzing, writing, and communicating information, which AI can effectively support through selection, text production, compilations, and decision-making material. At the same time, there are important human elements such as negotiation, trust-building, and assessments in organizational and labor law matters, particularly in a Swedish context with collective agreements and strong union presence. Overall, this results in a high but not maximal exposure to AI.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For Swedish HR specialists, there are clear current AI-adoption signals, but they are still mostly in early-to-mid adoption rather than fully mainstream. Observable use cases include AI-assisted drafting of job ads, screening CVs, summarizing interviews, generating HR policy text, employee Q&A chatbots, and support for workforce planning/analytics. These tools are available from major HRIS/recruitment vendors and are being adopted by many larger employers, especially in the private sector. However, core HR work in Sweden is constrained by labor-law sensitivity, union relations, privacy/GDPR, discrimination risk, and the need for human judgment in negotiations, restructuring, and case handling. Public-sector HR adoption is slower. Overall this looks like real but not universal deployment, with many organizations experimenting or using narrow point solutions rather than fully AI-native HR workflows.

descriptionEnglish: Provide information and advice to students regarding course selection, careers, and professional choices. Organise students' workplace practices.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that there will be little competition for jobs for study and career counselors in the coming year. This means that individuals with the required education or experience will have great opportunities for employment, although the job market may vary depending on the focus within the profession.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: Over a two-year period, the Swedish Public Employment Service estimates that the job prospects for study and career counselors will be significant. Employment opportunities in this field increase if you have relevant certifications, qualifications, or professional proof.
forecast.ingress: Det finns cirka 4700 yrkesverksamma studie- och yrkesvägledare i Sverige, varav cirka 81 procent är kvinnor och 19 procent är män.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Study and career counselors primarily work with information gathering, guidance, matching studies and professions, and planning internships. Most of the work is cognitive, documentation- and conversation-based, making it well-suited for AI support in terms of information retrieval, recommendations, scheduling, and drafting individual guidance. At the same time, the role requires human judgment, trust-building conversations, knowledge of students, and adaptation to the school's local context, which suggests that AI is likely to complement rather than replace the core tasks. In the Swedish public sector, regulatory frameworks and responsibilities may also complicate full automation, but the exposure to AI is still significant.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For Swedish study and career advisors, there are clear but still early signals of current adoption rather than mainstream deployment. Observable use cases include AI-assisted drafting of student communications, summarizing guidance notes, generating study/occupation comparisons, and helping staff search and organize information about education pathways and labor market trends. Some Swedish municipalities, schools, and education-adjacent platforms are experimenting with chatbots or AI support for general student services and administrative tasks, which can overlap with guidance work. However, the core advisory function remains strongly human-led, public sector adoption is slower, and there is little evidence of widespread, standardized AI tools embedded across the occupation. Overall, this fits the pattern of early adoption with some experimentation, but not yet significant mainstream use.

description: Undervisar elever med behov av särskilt stöd i förskola och förskoleklass samt grund- och gymnasieskola. descriptionEnglish: Teach students with special support needs in preschool and primary school as well as secondary school. Adapt teaching according to students' conditions and use specific aids and working methods. Provide special pedagogical support to teachers and develop teaching methodologies. Evaluate and supervise the application of education laws, syllabuses, and regulations.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that there will be very little competition for jobs for special education teachers and special educators in the coming year. forecast.mediumTermOutlook: In a two-year perspective, the Swedish Public Employment Service estimates that job opportunities for special education teachers and special educators will be very strong. Job opportunities in the profession increase if you have relevant certifications, qualifications, or professional licenses.
forecast.ingress: Det finns cirka 14200 yrkesverksamma speciallärare och specialpedagoger i Sverige, varav cirka 89 procent är kvinnor och 11 procent är män.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: The occupation is predominantly cognitive and knowledge-based: assessing students’ needs, adapting instruction, developing methods, advising teachers, and evaluating compliance with laws and curricula. These are tasks where AI could strongly assist with planning, documentation, analysis, individualized learning support, and drafting materials. However, the role also involves substantial human interaction, relationship-building, judgment in complex educational situations, and statutory responsibilities in Swedish schools that cannot be delegated to AI. Overall, it is a majority-digital/cognitive occupation with important interpersonal and institutional constraints, so exposure is high but not near-total.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For Swedish special teachers and special educators, current AI adoption signals are still mostly marginal-to-early. There are observable uses of generative AI and adaptive learning tools for lesson planning, differentiation, reading/writing support, and communication aids, but these are not yet mainstream occupational tools in schools. Adoption is constrained by public-sector procurement cycles, privacy/GDPR concerns, child-data sensitivity, uneven digital maturity across municipalities, and the fact that special education work is highly relational and case-specific. As of early 2026, the strongest signal is experimentation and selective use by individual schools/teachers rather than broad, standardized deployment across employers. So the occupation fits early adoption, not significant widespread usage.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation is highly screen- and knowledge-based: planning, investigations, report writing, drafting directives/regulations, and supervisory review are all text-heavy and decision-support tasks that AI systems can substantially assist with. The core work is mainly analytical, document-oriented, and information processing, with limited physical or hands-on components. In Sweden, legal accountability and public-sector procedures will slow full automation, but the task structure is still very exposed to AI.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For SSYK 2422, there are clear but still fairly uneven signals regarding AI usage today. The role includes planning, investigation, preparation of referral and decision-making materials, report writing, and supervision—tasks where generative AI tools are already being used for summarization, drafts, language review, information retrieval, and structuring. In the Swedish public sector and larger organizations, there is also an increase in advertisements for AI tools and internal pilot projects for case management, document production, and analytical support. At the same time, implementation is often limited by requirements for legal security, confidentiality, traceability, and low tolerance for errors, which means that usage has not yet been broadly standardized. Overall, this indicates early but real adoption rather than massive establishment, hence 5/10.

scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Lednings- och organisationsutvecklare are showing clear current AI-adoption signals, but not yet at the level of deeply embedded, occupation-specific automation. In 2026, many Swedish employers are using LLMs and AI tools for strategy support, process analysis, workshop preparation, policy drafting, benchmarking, survey/text analysis, and presentation work. This role overlaps heavily with knowledge work where AI is already practical and commonly experimented with. However, the occupation still depends on human judgment, stakeholder management, change leadership, and organization-specific context, so AI is more of an augmenting tool than a core workflow replacement. Adoption is stronger in larger private-sector firms and consulting environments, while public-sector and smaller employers lag. Overall this supports a score in the upper-middle range rather than top-tier mainstream automation.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Other economists primarily engage in analytical, rule-based, and knowledge-intensive work that often occurs digitally: budget and debt counseling, property valuation, economic assessments, and reports. Much of the core tasks can be partially supported by AI through data processing, forecasting, document review, and decision-making support. At the same time, there are important elements of client contact, advisory roles, assessments in specific cases, and sometimes regulatory or market contexts that require human judgment. Overall, the exposure is high but not maximal.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For SSYK 2419 (other economists), there are clear current AI signals, but they are still mostly in the early-adoption phase rather than broadly embedded. In practice, roles such as budget and debt advisory, economic investigation, forecasting, reporting, and property valuation can utilize LLMs and analytics tools for drafting texts, summarizing regulations, comparing documents, and supporting spreadsheet/workflow tasks. In Sweden, employers are increasingly mentioning AI literacy and the use of generative AI in economist/advisory-adjacent roles, especially within private sector finance, property, consulting, and larger municipalities. However, the occupation is heterogeneous; many tasks involve judgment, local regulations, and accountability, and public-sector adoption is slower. Thus, the observable current adoption is real but not yet mainstream across the occupation, aligning with an early-to-mid adoption level.

nameEnglish: Economists and Macro Analysts and Others
description: Studerar, analyserar, utvecklar och förklarar ekonomiska drivkrafter utifrån nationalekonomiska teorier och modeller. Jämför samhällsekonomiska teorier och modeller för att analysera och prognostisera beteenden och fenomen på individ-, företags- och samhällsnivå exempelvis arbetslöshet, inflation, investeringar och ekonomisk tillväxt. Analyserar stora världshändelser och länders förhållanden. Föreslår och värderar utfallet av politiska reformer och åtgärder. Utvecklar nya ekonomiska modeller.
descriptionEnglish: Study, analyse, develop and explain economic drivers based on macroeconomic theories and models. Compare socio-economic theories and models to analyse and predict behaviours and phenomena on individual, company, and societal levels, such as unemployment, inflation, investments, and economic growth. Analyze major global events and countries' relations. Propose and evaluate the outcomes of political reforms and measures. Develop new economic models.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: No forecast available.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: No forecast available.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: The occupation is almost entirely knowledge- and screen/computer-based. The core tasks consist of collecting and interpreting data, building and using economic models, analyzing scenarios, forecasting developments, and writing reports or policy briefs. This is precisely the type of text, analytical, and decision-support work where AI excels. There are certain human elements such as policy understanding, judgment, and communication with clients, but these do not change the fact that the majority of the work is highly exposed to AI.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: This occupation shows clear current AI adoption signals, but not yet near full mainstream, heavily integrated use. For economists and macro analysts, LLMs and analytical AI tools are commonly used for drafting briefs, summarizing research, scanning large document sets, coding/cleaning data, and supporting scenario analysis or forecasting workflows. In Sweden, employers in finance, consulting, central banks, ministries, and research organizations increasingly mention AI/data tools in analyst roles, and many professionals are likely using them informally or as productivity aids. However, the core work still relies on human judgment, model selection, interpretation, and policy assessment, and public-sector and high-stakes forecasting environments remain cautious. So the current observable adoption level is significant but not yet uniformly embedded across the occupation.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Traders and fund managers work almost entirely with digital and knowledge-intensive tasks: analyzing markets, monitoring investments, building strategies, making decisions, overseeing results, and revising plans. scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For traders and fund managers, there are clear, observable signals of AI adoption today. In the Swedish and Nordic financial sectors, there is also an increasing demand for AI/data skills in relevant roles, and larger asset managers and banks have begun integrating AI support into analysis and decision-making processes. At the same time, core decisions are still strongly human-driven due to accountability, regulations, and market risk, so AI does not replace the role but primarily serves as decision support. This makes the adoption clear and broad enough for 6–7, rather than just at the pilot level.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that there will be competition for jobs for controllers in the coming year. This means that individuals with the required education or experience will have small to medium employment opportunities, but the job market may vary depending on the focus within the profession.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: Over a two-year period, the Swedish Public Employment Service estimates that job prospects for controllers will be significant. Employment opportunities in this field increase if you have relevant certifications, qualifications, or professional proof.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Controller work is highly screen- and knowledge-based and centered on analysis, forecasting, reporting, follow-up, and developing planning/monitoring systems. These tasks map well to AI strengths in data processing, pattern detection, drafting, scenario analysis, and decision support. The role is not fully automatable because it also requires judgment, business context, coordination with management, and tailoring to organizational and regulatory conditions, but the core task structure is strongly exposed to AI.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For Swedish controllers, there are clear current adoption signals for AI, but not yet at levels 8–10. In practice, AI/LLM tools are increasingly used for budgeting and forecasting, variance analysis, report drafting, aggregation of key figures, scenario analysis, and support in monthly/quarterly reporting. There are also many commercial BI and FP&A platforms with built-in AI functionalities that are already being adopted by larger employers. At the same time, the role remains heavily dependent on internal data, control models, business understanding, and quality assurance, which means that adoption is often selective and human-driven rather than fully automated. In the Swedish context, this is particularly evident: larger private companies and corporations clearly show usage patterns, while the public sector and smaller organizations lag behind. Overall, this is more than early experimentation but not yet fully comprehensive, therefore a 6/10.

This means that individuals with the required education or experience will have small to medium employment opportunities, but the job market may vary depending on the focus within the profession.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: Over a two-year period, the Swedish Public Employment Service estimates that job prospects for auditors will be significant. Employment opportunities in this field increase if you have relevant certifications, qualifications, or professional proof.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Auditors primarily work with digital, knowledge-intensive analysis of accounting, internal control, budgeting, taxation, and financial management. A large part of the work involves reviewing documents, identifying discrepancies, compiling information, drawing conclusions, and providing advice—tasks where AI excels. There are certain elements of professional judgment, client contact, accountability, and regular interpretation of Swedish accounting and tax law that require human sign-off, but the core tasks are clearly screen- and information-based. Therefore, the exposure is very high.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For Swedish accountants/auditors (SSYK 2411), there are clear current adoption signals but not universal AI-native workflows. Observable use cases include AI-assisted document review, anomaly detection in bookkeeping, invoice/expense matching, drafting client communications, and research support for tax/accounting questions in major audit and advisory firms. Big international and Swedish firms have been publicly promoting AI-enabled audit analytics and automation in assurance and advisory, and finance teams increasingly use embedded AI in ERP/accounting platforms. However, core audit judgment, statutory sign-off, and public-sector/regulated workflows still rely heavily on human oversight, and adoption is uneven across smaller firms and municipal/state employers. Overall this fits significant current tooling with many employers adopting, but not yet near-ubiquitous end-to-end AI use.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that there will be very little competition for jobs for preschool teachers in the coming year. This means that individuals with the required education or experience will have very good opportunities for work, although the job market may vary depending on the specific field within the profession.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: In a two-year perspective, the Swedish Public Employment Service estimates that job opportunities for preschool teachers will be very strong. Job opportunities in the profession increase if you have relevant certifications, qualifications, or professional licenses.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Preschool teachers engage heavily in planning, pedagogical documentation, language support, observations, and designing instruction according to the curriculum, which provides clear exposure to AI in the cognitive domains. At the same time, the core of the profession is strongly relational and context-dependent: presence with the children, fostering security, conflict resolution, care, and ongoing adaptation within the group require human interaction and physical presence. In Swedish preschools, the delegation of pedagogical and caregiving responsibilities is also restricted by regulations and collective practices. Overall: significant but not high exposure.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Preschool teachers today have some clear AI signals, but they are primarily indirect and early rather than broadly integrated into core work. In Swedish preschools, tools such as generative writing assistants are used for planning, documentation, communication with guardians, and simpler compilations, but this seems to be limited to individual teams, municipalities, or pilot-like implementations. There is also some use of AI in educational apps and support tools, but few observable signs that AI is a standardized part of teaching, assessment, or operational management in preschools. Since the public sector in Sweden adopts innovation more slowly and the work of preschool teachers is heavily based on relationships and care, the current adoption is assessed as marginally early: 3/10.

description: Undervisar på gymnasial och eftergymnasial nivå i yrkesämnen. Planerar, genomför och utvärderar undervisningen i enlighet med läro- och ämnesplaner. Utför bedömning och betygsättning.
descriptionEnglish: Teach at secondary and post-secondary levels in vocational subjects. Plan, implement, and evaluate teaching in accordance with curricula and subject syllabuses. Conduct assessment and grading.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that there will be very little competition for jobs for vocational teachers in the coming year. This means that individuals with the required education or experience will have very good opportunities for work, although the job market may vary depending on the specific field within the profession.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: In a two-year perspective, the Swedish Public Employment Service estimates that job opportunities for vocational teachers will be very strong. Job opportunities in the profession increase if you have relevant certifications, qualifications, or professional licenses.
forecast.ingress: Det finns cirka 10400 yrkesverksamma lärare i yrkesämnen i Sverige, varav cirka 43 procent är kvinnor och 57 procent är män.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Vocational teachers largely work with knowledge-intensive and digital tasks: planning of lessons, creation of materials, assessment, grading, and follow-up. AI can therefore significantly impact core tasks, particularly through support for lesson planning, exam questions, feedback, and administrative assessment work. At the same time, there are important human and context-specific elements such as practice-oriented mentoring, relationship building, student adjustment, and formal grading according to Swedish regulations, which limit full automation. Overall, the exposure is high but not maximal.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For Teachers in Vocational Subjects, there are clear observable AI signals in 2025/early 2026, but they are still mostly early-stage rather than mainstream. Many Swedish schools and vocational training providers are experimenting with generative AI for lesson planning, differentiated materials, quiz/test generation, feedback drafting, and administrative support. Teachers also increasingly encounter AI through student use, requiring guidance on source criticism, assessment integrity, and adapted assignments. However, for this occupation there is limited evidence that AI is yet deeply embedded in core teaching workflows across employers, and public-sector education adoption remains uneven and cautious. In practice, current use looks like scattered pilots and local experimentation rather than broad standardized deployment, so the occupation fits the 4–5 range.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: PhD students primarily work with knowledge production, literature reviews, analysis, writing, data collection, interpretation, and presentation of results, which is very well suited for AI support. They also participate in teaching, but that aspect is usually smaller than their research work and includes elements that require human judgment and interaction. Overall, the profession is almost entirely screen- and knowledge-based, with high theoretical exposure to AI.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Doktorander (PhD students) show clear early-adoption signals for AI, but not yet broad, standardized deployment in the occupation. Observable use in 2026 typically includes LLMs for literature searches, summarizing papers, coding/data analysis support, drafting emails and reports, language editing, and help with teaching materials. In Swedish universities, many doctoral students use AI tools informally or through institution-sanctioned generic tools, but usage is uneven across fields and supervisors, and policies around research integrity, citations, data privacy, and thesis authorship keep adoption cautious. There are also strong discipline differences: more visible use in computational, quantitative, and engineering-oriented doctoral work; more limited or constrained use in humanities, education, and some social-science contexts. Overall this fits early adoption rather than mainstream occupational standardization.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Research assistants plan, conduct, and lead research activities, as well as participate in teaching. The core of the work is heavily knowledge, analysis, and writing-intensive: literature reviews, research design, data interpretation, reporting, applications, and teaching materials are tasks where AI can provide significant support. scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Research assistants and similar academic research roles show clear, observable AI signals in 2026 but not at levels indicative of widespread, standardized usage across all employers. At the same time, usage is very uneven among institutions, research fields, and projects, with much work still relying on human methodology, quality, and research assessment. Additionally, the public sector and academia often have more cautious policies regarding data, copyright, and ethics. Overall, this points to early but real adoption, rather than mainstream: approximately 5/10.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Optometry work is clearly mixed. A significant part of the core is knowledge- and decision-based: eye exams, evaluation of vision problems, selection of glasses/contact lenses, and consultation can partially be supported by AI through analysis, recommendations, and record systems. At the same time, it includes practical work with fitting, adjustment, and repair of glasses as well as direct customer contact, which requires physical presence and human interaction. Therefore, the exposure is moderate rather than high.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For opticians in Sweden, there are clear AI-enabling tools on the market, but current observable adoption appears to be early rather than mainstream. In practice, optical retail and clinic workflows are still dominated by established equipment, human fitting, sales, and manual clinical judgment. Some vendors globally market AI for refraction support, retinal screening, frame recommendation, appointment/chat automation, and customer-service triage, but these are not yet widely visible as standard practice across Swedish optiker employers. Compared with occupations where AI is already embedded in daily work, signals here suggest a few pilots and selective use by larger chains or tech-forward practices, rather than broad deployment. So this sits around early adoption, not significant adoption.

description: Utreder och bedömer hörselbesvär och anpassar hörhjälpmedel. Informerar och utbildar om hörselsvårigheter. Undersöker, ställer diagnos och behandlar röst-, tal- och språkstörningar. Leder träning och ordinerar kommunikationshjälpmedel.
descriptionEnglish: Investigates and assesses hearing difficulties and adapts hearing aids. Informs and educates about hearing impairments. Examines, diagnoses, and treats voice, speech, and language disorders. Leads training and prescribes communication aids.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: No forecast available.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: No forecast available.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Audiologists and speech therapists have a majority of their core tasks in assessment, diagnostics, documentation, education, and treatment planning, all of which involve cognitive and digital work where AI can provide significant support. Examples include analysis of hearing data, assistance in differential assessment, record-keeping, patient information, and suggestions for training or assistive device arrangements. At the same time, the profession requires substantial direct patient contact, clinical examinations, adaptation of assistive devices, and ongoing assessment of speech, voice, and language difficulties in real situations. In the Swedish healthcare and rehabilitation context, the delegation of many decisions and interventions is also limited by professional responsibility and regulations. Overall, the exposure is high but not quite maximal.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For audionomer och logopeder there are clear but still limited current AI-adoption signals in Sweden as of early 2026. In hearing care, AI is increasingly present in hearing-aid fitting software, speech-in-noise analysis, remote fine-tuning, and some automated audiogram/usage interpretation tools, but these are usually embedded in vendor platforms rather than broadly reshaping practice. In speech-language pathology, AI is visible in speech transcription, documentation support, language/speech screening aids, and some therapy apps, yet most clinicians still rely on standard clinical workflows and validated non-AI methods. Swedish public-sector settings likely adopt more slowly due to procurement, privacy, and clinical-evidence requirements. Overall this looks like early but real adoption rather than mainstream AI-heavy use.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Pharmacists primarily work with knowledge-intensive and digital tasks: drug information, control, advice, marketing, research, and documentation. Much of the core work consists of interpreting, compiling, and communicating medical information, which AI can support effectively. At the same time, there are important elements of professional responsibility, patient safety, regulatory assessment, and human counseling that limit full automation. In Sweden, regulations and collective agreements can also slow down the implementation, but the nature of the work itself is clearly highly exposed.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For pharmacists, there are clear yet still limited current AI signals in Sweden. In practice, AI is primarily evident in drug information, customer/patient support, internal knowledge search, text processing, and some quality assurance/regulatory work among larger pharmacy operators and pharmaceutical companies. There are also general generative AI tools used for summarization, communication, and documentation, but they are not as deeply integrated into core operations as they are, for example, in software development or certain imaging diagnostic specialties. At the same time, the pharmacy and pharmaceutical sector is heavily regulated, which slows down broad, standardized deployment in daily workflows. Overall, this suggests early but genuine adoption: multiple use cases, some actual usage, but not yet mainstream across the entire profession.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Occupational therapists have a distinctly mixed work profile. A significant portion of the job is cognitive and digital: assessing activity ability, documentation, planning interventions, selecting and adapting aids, as well as working with IT-based support and environmental adjustments. These aspects are relatively exposed to AI, particularly for information searches, suggestions for aids, journal support, and structuring of action plans. At the same time, the core of the profession is heavily reliant on personal meetings, observation in real-life environments, practical adjustments, collaboration with patients, relatives, and other professionals, as well as assessments in complex care and support contexts. In the Swedish welfare context, direct automation is furthermore limited by governmental and organizational requirements. Overall, the exposure is moderate rather than high.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Occupational therapists are showing clear but still early signs of adoption rather than widespread, established use of AI. There are already observable tools and applications: AI support in documentation and record-keeping, speech-to-text, decision support regarding aids, planning and communication, as well as digital rehabilitation and training platforms with AI components. In the Swedish context, this is primarily found in larger regions, private care providers, and in certain assistive technology/rehabilitation environments, but not as a standard across the entire profession. At the same time, the public sector is slower, and for occupational therapy, there are still no strong signals that AI is deeply integrated into core activities in the same way as in more data- or text-intensive professions. Overall: more than marginal pilot level, but still clearly ahead of mainstream — approximately 4.5/10.

Restores function and alleviates pain using manual techniques like manipulation, stretching, and massage.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: No forecast available.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: No forecast available.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Chiropractors and naprapaths have a clearly hands-on professional role where the core of their work involves examination, palpation, treatment, and manual techniques such as manipulation, stretching, and massage. At the same time, there are knowledge-based components involved, including taking medical history, assessment, record-keeping, treatment planning, and patient information, where AI can provide some support. However, physical and personal interaction dominates, and many central aspects cannot be replaced by AI. Overall, the exposure level is moderately low to medium, around 4.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For kiropraktorer and naprapater in Sweden, there are some observable AI-related signals, but they appear marginal rather than widespread. Current AI use is more likely limited to generic administrative tools (booking, documentation support, marketing, patient communication) than core clinical work. The occupation is highly hands-on and relies on physical examination, manual treatment, and patient interaction, which limits direct AI integration. There is no strong evidence of mainstream AI-assisted diagnostics or treatment workflows being broadly deployed across Swedish clinics as of early 2026. Overall, this looks like early, small-scale experimentation rather than significant adoption.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Dentistry has a clearly knowledge-intensive core where AI can support diagnostics, image interpretation, treatment planning, record-keeping, patient information, and evidence searching. At the same time, the profession is highly hands-on and requires physical examination, manual interventions, surgery, adaptation to the patient's anatomy, and ongoing clinical responsibility in real-time. The advisory and preventive aspects are more digitizable than the operational treatments. Overall, it is a mixed profession with significant but not predominant exposure to AI.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For Swedish dentists, there are clear but still limited current AI adoption signals as of early 2026. AI is observable in dental imaging support, radiology/CBCT interpretation aids, treatment planning, appointment/admin automation, and some CAD/CAM and workflow software used in clinics. Large vendors and some private chains market AI-assisted tools, and there are pilots or selective use in parts of the sector. However, this is not yet a broadly routine, occupation-wide standard in Sweden: clinical diagnosis and treatment remain mainly human-led, and adoption is uneven across private vs. public dentistry and across clinic sizes. So the occupation looks like early adoption rather than mainstream deployment.

description: Undersöker, ställer diagnos och behandlar sjuka och skadade djur. Ger råd om djurhälsovård och avel. Kontrollerar djurbesättningar och slaktdjur. Ger råd och kontrollerar livsmedelshygien, djurskydd, smittskydd och import av djur och livsmedel.
descriptionEnglish: Examines, diagnoses, and treats sick and injured animals. Advises on animal health care and breeding. Inspects animal herds and livestock. Provides advice and supervises food hygiene, animal welfare, infection control, and the import of animals and food.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: No forecast available.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: No forecast available.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Veterinarians have a clearly mixed work profile. A large part of the job is knowledge-intensive and digital: taking anamnesis, assessing symptoms, formulating differential diagnoses, maintaining records, providing advice, offering referral/decision support, performing infection control assessments, and overseeing food hygiene and animal welfare. These aspects are relatively exposed to AI, particularly in terms of decision support, documentation, and information retrieval. At the same time, core tasks require physical examination, sample collection, treatment, practical animal work, and often on-site visits to farms, herds, and slaughterhouses, which AI cannot replace. In a Swedish context, the delegation of certain regulatory and supervision tasks is also limited. Overall, the exposure is medium to quite high, but not dominated by pure screen tasks.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For veterinarians in Sweden, there are some observable AI signals, but they are still limited and not yet mainstream. Current tools are more likely to appear in adjacent workflows such as image analysis support, clinical documentation, scheduling, triage chatbots, and administrative automation within larger animal hospitals or veterinary chains. However, core veterinary diagnosis and treatment remain heavily dependent on direct clinical expertise, hands-on examination, and regulatory/accountability constraints. Compared with human healthcare specialties, there is far less visible evidence of broad, routine AI deployment in everyday veterinary practice. As of early 2026 this looks like marginal-to-early adoption rather than significant widespread usage.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Psychotherapists primarily work with conversation, assessment, documentation, and treatment planning, meaning knowledge- and text/decision-intensive tasks that AI can support effectively. At the same time, the profession requires strong human relationships, trust, clinical intuition, and responsibility in interactions with individuals, families, and groups, which limits full automation. In the Swedish healthcare and social care context, it is also challenging to replace the professional role, but digital support for record-keeping, screening, formulations, and treatment suggestions still provides significant exposure.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For psychotherapists, there are clear but still limited current-adoption signals in Sweden as of early 2026. AI is mainly used as support around clinical work rather than in the therapy itself: documentation, summarizing conversations, administrative documentation, triage/chatbots, and sometimes decision support in digital care workflows. There is also a growing range of evidence-based digital treatment platforms, and some organizations are testing AI to streamline intake and follow-up. At the same time, direct AI-supported psychotherapeutic work is still uncommon, heavily regulated, and more cautious in the public sector and licensed care. Overall, this indicates early adoption/pilots rather than broad, established use.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Radiology nurses have a mix of clinical work and digital/assessment-intensive tasks. AI can assist with image pre-review, protocol suggestions, quality assurance, documentation, and prioritization, but the core of the job still involves managing patients, positioning them, monitoring examinations, and checking medical equipment on-site. In the Swedish healthcare environment, the delegation of many tasks is also limited by regulations, responsibilities, and collectively organized work. Overall, this results in a medium level of exposure.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Radiology nurses operate in a medically regulated and patient-facing environment where AI is already present as support in certain radiology workflows, but adoption remains limited and uneven across Sweden. There are clear current signals for AI in imaging diagnostics: triage, finding detection, prioritization of examinations, image quality control, and decision support in radiology are being used or piloted in several healthcare providers and regions. At the same time, the role of the radiology nurse is less directly automated by AI than that of the radiologist, and practical use often concerns workflow support rather than replacing or reshaping job tasks. In Swedish public healthcare, implementation often progresses slowly due to procurement, IT integration, accountability, evidence requirements, and patient safety. In the Swedish healthcare context, delegation is also limited for governmental, ethical, and union reasons. Overall, exposure is moderate rather than high.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For SSYK 2219 (other physicians), there are clear and observable AI signals in Swedish and international clinical practice, but adoption is not yet fully pervasive across the medical profession. What indicates a relatively high level is that AI support is used in workflows close to diagnosis and triage, medical record summarization, dictation/transcription, decision support, and image interpretation in certain specialties and healthcare organizations. In Sweden, there is also an increasing use of medical AI tools in regions, private providers, and specialty areas, although this varies significantly between organizations. At the same time, this is a broad category of physicians where much work still requires human judgment, responsibility, and regulatory caution; public healthcare also tends to progress more slowly than private actors. Overall, current signals indicate significant yet still uneven adoption, which justifies a rating of 7 rather than 8–10.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Work environment engineers and occupational/environmental hygienists primarily engage in knowledge-intensive work: inspections, risk assessments, data analysis, documentation, investigations, and recommendations. scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For occupational health engineers/occupational and environmental hygienists, there are clear AI-adjacent tools in use, but current adoption looks early rather than mainstream. Observable use cases as of early 2026 include AI-assisted document drafting for inspection reports, policy summaries, checklist generation, and analysis of sensor/measurement data (noise, air quality, exposure logs). Some employers in occupational health, larger industrial firms, and consultancies likely use LLMs or analytics tools to speed up reporting and risk assessment. However, the core work is still heavily physical, regulated, and case-specific: workplace inspections, measurements, legal compliance assessments, and recommendations require human judgment and on-site verification. In Sweden, public-sector and regulated safety work tends to adopt slower, and there are limited strong signals of occupation-specific, widely deployed AI systems comparable to those in software or customer service. So the best fit is early adoption with some experimentation and partial workflow support, not broad deployment.

descriptionEnglish: Designs, shapes, and develops digital and electronic media. scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: The profession is heavily digital and knowledge-intensive: it involves designing, shaping, and developing games and digital media, creating special effects, animations, and moving images, as well as defining design goals and restrictions. The tasks primarily consist of creative conceptual work, visual production, and digital iteration, which closely aligns with what generative AI tools can already support in areas such as image, animation, idea development, and prototyping. There is some human and client interaction, but it does not constitute the majority of the work. Therefore, the exposure to AI is assessed as very high.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For Swedish game and digital media designers, there are clear current adoption signals as of early 2026, but not yet universal deployment. AI image/video generation, concept art assistance, motion/animation tooling, rapid ideation, localization, and asset iteration are actively used in parts of the games, marketing, and media industries. Job ads and workplace discussions increasingly mention generative AI familiarity, prompt-based workflows, and experience with AI-assisted creative tools. However, adoption is uneven across studios and agencies: many teams still use AI selectively for ideation, mockups, background assets, or post-production support rather than as a core production pipeline. Concerns about style control, IP rights, quality, and brand consistency also limit full-scale use. Overall this fits significant but still partial current adoption, not yet near-universal mainstream penetration.

scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Graphic designers are already clearly using AI in practice, but the adoption is not as pervasive as in pure text or code professions. Many agencies, in-house teams, and freelancers use AI for brainstorming, image variations, retouching, copy suggestions, and mockups. At the same time, the profession still requires a high level of human control for brand, typography, originality, and client dialogue, which means that AI primarily serves as a work support rather than a full replacement. Overall: significant and growing actual usage, but not yet completely mainstream among all employers.

descriptionEnglish: Handles technical, economic, and legal issues related to property formation. Responsible for valuation and registration of properties. Oversees measurement and mapping work and the creation and updating of maps. Analyses aerial and satellite images.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: No forecast available.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: No forecast available.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Land surveyors work extensively with knowledge- and computer-based tasks: property formation, valuation, registration, cartography, analysis of aerial and satellite images, as well as legal/technical advice. Much of this is something that AI can strongly support through information retrieval, image analysis, document review, decision support, and automated cartographic and data management. At the same time, there are elements that require regulatory authority, professional judgment, local knowledge, cooperation with parties, and sometimes site visits/measuring work, which means that the work is not entirely digital. Overall, the exposure is high, but not maximal.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For cadastral surveyors, there are clear but still limited current adoption signals rather than widespread, established AI usage. In Sweden, AI tools are primarily used to support cartographic analysis, image interpretation, document/case management, and some automatic quality control, which aligns with parts of surveyors' work involving aerial and satellite imagery, property data, and documentation. At the same time, the profession is heavily regulated, often publicly organized, and reliant on legal and technical accountability, which hinders full-scale AI implementation. Therefore, it is likely that there are individual pilot projects and selective use by certain authorities and consulting firms, but not yet a broad standard tool within the profession. In summary: clear early adoption, but not mainstream.

Overall, there are clear but still early to moderate signs of adoption, thus placing it around the middle of the scale.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: No forecast available.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: No forecast available.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: The profession is largely knowledge and analysis-based: development of theories and methods, data analysis, evaluation, and the creation of practical applications within agronomy, the food supply chain, and horticulture. These activities align closely with areas where AI can provide strong support, particularly in information retrieval, modeling, data analysis, and decision support. At the same time, there are components involving field trials, consulting with stakeholders, and the need for site and context-specific assessments, which means it is not entirely digital. Overall, there is high but not maximal exposure to AI.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For Swedish agronomists and agricultural advisers (SSYK 2134), there are clear AI-enabled tools in use today, but the observable signal is still mostly early-stage rather than mainstream. In practice, current adoption shows up in precision agriculture platforms, satellite/drone image analysis, decision-support systems for crop disease/pest detection, yield prediction, and data analysis in farm advisory work. Larger agribusinesses, input suppliers, and research-heavy employers are more likely to use LLMs and analytics tools for drafting reports, summarizing trial results, and processing field data. However, compared with occupations like software, marketing, or customer support, there is less evidence of broad, standardized AI deployment across the profession. Many Swedish advisers still rely on domain software, sensors, and statistical tools with AI as an add-on rather than a core workflow. So the strongest fit is early adoption with some real deployment, not yet significant mainstream adoption.

description: Utvecklar teorier, modeller och metoder inom farmakologi. Genomför försök, analyserar och värderar information om hur läkemedel och andra ämnen verkar i celler, organ, djur och människor, samt vilken effekt de har. Utvecklar och formar praktiska tillämpningar inom farmaci och medicin. Informerar, ger råd och utfärdar anvisningar om läkemedel.
descriptionEnglish: Develops theories, models, and methods in pharmacology. Conducts experiments, analyzes, and evaluates information on how drugs and other substances act in cells, organs, animals, and humans, as well as their effects. Develops and shapes practical applications in pharmacy and medicine. Provides information, advice, and instructions about medications.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: No forecast available.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: No forecast available.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Pharmacologists and biomedicine researchers engage extensively in knowledge-intensive, digital, and analytical tasks: developing theories and models, analyzing experimental data, evaluating information, writing briefs, and making recommendations regarding pharmaceuticals. These tasks present clear opportunities for AI to support literature reviews, data analysis, hypothesis generation, reporting, and decision-making. At the same time, there are significant aspects that require human expertise, responsibility, laboratory and experimental work, as well as professional assessment of safety and efficacy. Overall, the exposure is high but not complete.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For farmakologer och biomedicinare, there are clear current AI signals, but they look more like early-stage augmentation than broad, routine deployment. In 2026, AI is increasingly used in adjacent research and life-science workflows for literature screening, molecule/drug discovery, image analysis, lab data processing, and bioinformatics, and Swedish universities, hospitals, and pharma/medtech employers are likely exposing staff to these tools. However, the occupation’s core work still depends heavily on wet-lab experimentation, regulatory judgment, clinical/scientific validation, and human responsibility, which limits full automation. Observable adoption is strongest in research-intensive and larger private-sector settings, while public-sector biomedical environments tend to be slower. Overall this fits early adoption with some employers experimenting and a growing set of tools, but not yet mainstream, hence around 4.5/10.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Plant and animal biologists largely work with knowledge-intensive and digital tasks: theory development, modeling, method development, species identification, analysis of distribution and behaviors, as well as quality assurance of analytical methods. Studerar funktionerna hos olika delar av arvsmassa. Utvecklar och kvalitetssäkrar mikro- och molekylärbiologiska analysmetoder.

Hit hör inte 
Farmakolog (2133)
Biomedicinare (2133)
descriptionEnglish: Develops theories, models, and methods that address the components and structure of cells. Collects and analyzes cell samples to study how molecules and systems within the cell interact, how various substances affect cell development, and how these processes are regulated. Studies the functions of different parts of genetic material. Develops and ensures quality in micro- and molecular biological analytical methods.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: No forecast available.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: No forecast available.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: The work is primarily knowledge- and analysis-intensive: developing theories, models, and methods, analyzing cell samples, interpreting molecular interactions, and ensuring the quality of analytical methods. Much of the core consists of digital/theoretical work that AI can support well, such as literature reviews, data analysis, pattern recognition, method development, and reporting. At the same time, there are certain laboratory and quality assurance aspects that require physical presence, experimental skill, and responsibility for sample management, which detracts from maximum exposure. In a Swedish context, regulatory and organizational requirements may also hinder full AI automation, but the theoretical exposure remains high.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For cell- and molecular biologists in Sweden, there are clear current AI-adoption signals, but they are still mostly early-stage rather than mainstream. In practice, AI/ML is being used in parts of bioinformatics, image analysis (microscopy/pathology-adjacent workflows), omics analysis, and lab automation/data interpretation, especially in research-heavy universities, life-science firms, and larger pharma/biotech employers. However, the occupation itself is not broadly defined by routine use of dedicated AI tools in the way software development or customer support are. Most job ads and workplace signals point to support for analysis, method development, and research workflows rather than widespread “AI-first” execution. In Sweden, adoption is likely stronger in private R&D and weaker in public/academic lab environments, so the overall current adoption level is best placed in the early-adoption range.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: No forecast available.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Statisticians primarily engage in knowledge- and screen-based tasks: developing statistical methods, data collection/processing, analysis, forecasting, and presenting results. The core of the profession consists of text, data, and decision-making work where AI is well-suited to automate or assist with coding, analysis, visualization, report writing, and forecasting support. There are certain elements of quality assurance, method selection, and communication with clients that require human expertise, but these do not change the fact that the profession has a very high level of theoretical and digital exposure.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Statistiker in Sweden show strong current AI-adoption signals, though not at the level of fully standardized AI-native professions. However, for SSYK 2122 the role is still fundamentally human-led and method-driven, and in public sector/statistical authority settings adoption is slower due to governance, quality, explainability, and procurement constraints. descriptionEnglish: Develops theories, models, and methods, and designs practical applications in chemistry. Leads experiments and analyzes and evaluates data in a laboratory environment. Provides information, advice, and issues directives and permits. Develops and tests chemical products and processes. Prepares documentation and handles registration matters.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Employment Service assesses that there will be competition for jobs for chemists in the coming year. This means that individuals with the required education or experience will have small to medium opportunities for employment, but the job market may vary depending on the specific field within the profession.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: In two years, the Employment Service projects that job opportunities for chemists will be significant. Employment prospects within the profession improve if you have relevant certifications, qualifications, or professional credentials.
forecast.ingress: Det finns cirka 3200 yrkesverksamma kemister i Sverige, varav cirka 54 procent är kvinnor och 46 procent är män.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Chemists primarily work with knowledge-intensive tasks: developing theories, analyzing data, interpreting experiments, writing documentation, providing advice, and managing registration matters. These aspects are relatively exposed to AI, as AI can support literature reviews, data analysis, modeling, report writing, and certain parts of regulatory and documentation work. At the same time, there are important components that require laboratory work, experimental design, practical assessment, responsibility for safety and quality, as well as professional review expertise that cannot be easily automated. Overall, the profession is clearly digital/cognitive but not entirely screen-based, resulting in high but not maximal exposure.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Kemister (SSYK 2113) show clear but still uneven current AI adoption signals in early 2026. There is observable use of AI/ML in chemistry-adjacent work such as spectral analysis, lab data interpretation, formulation support, literature search, and document drafting for regulatory/registration tasks. Some Swedish employers in pharma, materials, food, and industrial chemistry are likely using LLMs or analytics tools in R&D and QA workflows, especially for summarizing reports, searching prior art, and assisting with experiment planning. However, this is not yet a mainstream, occupation-wide transformation: much chemistry work remains constrained by wet-lab reality, validation requirements, safety/regulatory accountability, and specialized instrumentation/software that still depends heavily on human expertise. So the best fit is early adoption with a meaningful number of pilots and selective deployment, but not broad standardized use across employers.

descriptionEnglish: Processes and analyzes collected data on air currents, air pressure, temperature, humidity, physical and chemical changes in pollutants, and other phenomena. Prepares and reports short- or long-term weather forecasts. Conducts investigations about the atmosphere and climate.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: No forecast available.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: No forecast available.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Meteorologists primarily work with processing, analyzing, and interpreting digital data, as well as writing forecasts and investigations about weather and climate. It is a highly cognitive, screen- and knowledge-intensive role where AI is already strong in data analysis, pattern recognition, forecasting support, and report generation. At the same time, the work requires human expert judgment, responsibility for interpretation, quality assurance, and communication of forecasts, which makes it likely that AI will complement rather than completely replace the profession. Some field and observational elements exist, but the core tasks are clearly digital and analytical.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Current AI adoption for meteorologists in Sweden appears to be in early adoption rather than mainstream. There are clear observable signals of AI use in weather/climate work: modern forecasting increasingly relies on ML-enhanced post-processing, pattern recognition, nowcasting, satellite/radar interpretation, and automated decision support built into meteorological software. However, these are typically specialized tools embedded in forecasting systems rather than broad, visible AI tool adoption by the occupation itself. In Swedish public-sector and public-service contexts, deployment is generally slower and more regulated, and there is little strong evidence that most meteorologists are using general-purpose LLM tools as a core part of daily work. Overall, the occupation shows real but limited current AI adoption signals, best placed around 4–5.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Physicists and astronomers primarily perform cognitive, analytical, and digital tasks: they develop theories and models, analyze data, perform calculations, interpret experiments, and write documentation, guidelines, and permits. At the same time, there are elements of research management, experimental design, physical measurement activities, and professional responsibility that require human expertise and limit full automation. scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Physicists and astronomers show clear signals of AI adoption, but it remains primarily early and selective rather than widespread. There are also concrete tools and platforms that can support researchers and engineers; however, I do not see the same level of standardized, routine AI usage as in, for example, software development or customer service. In the Swedish context, adoption is likely occurring within universities, research institutes, space/measurement technology, and larger industry-related laboratories, but not as a universal norm in the profession. Overall, there are clear yet still limited and uneven current adoption signals, placing the profession in a stage of early adoption.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Managers in retail primarily focus on goal and strategy formulation, monitoring, budgeting, resource allocation, and personnel responsibility. It is largely a digital and knowledge-based leadership role where AI can support analysis, forecasting, reporting, scheduling, budget tracking, and decision-making materials. At the same time, there are important human elements such as leadership, accountability, negotiation, and HR issues that cannot be fully automated. Overall, the theoretical exposure to AI is high, but not maximal.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For SSYK 1730 (managers in trade), there are clear but still fairly limited current AI signals. In practice, generative AI tools and analytical support are used for tasks such as forecasting, assortment/inventory analysis, campaign planning, meeting materials, reporting, and personnel administrative texts. At the same time, the role is broad and close to operations, and many decisions require context, responsibility, and human judgement rather than full automation. Therefore, there are more signs of experimentation and selective use than of widespread, standardized AI integration in the management role itself. Overall, it fits best in the early adoption phase, approximately 4–5.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Restaurant and kitchen managers primarily work with planning, budgeting, personnel responsibility, monitoring, goals, and strategies—many administrative and decision-support tasks that AI can significantly influence. At the same time, the role requires ongoing human leadership, presence in the operation, conflict management, staffing, and adaptation to the kitchen and restaurant environment, which limits full automation. Overall, the exposure is high but not maximal: mostly cognitive/digital support with important human elements.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For restaurant and kitchen managers, there are clear but still limited current AI signals in Sweden. The most observable applications relate to scheduling, staffing planning, purchasing/forecasting, menu design, recipe and cost optimization, as well as analysis of guest data and reviews in larger chains and hotel groups. There are also many general AI tools for administration, meeting notes, HR, and communication that can be utilized by managers in the profession. At the same time, this is not a profession with widespread, standardized AI implementation in core operations at the same level as, for example, customer service or office-based analytical roles. In practice, adoption seems to be early and uneven: some employers are experimenting with or using niche tools, but it is not yet mainstream for the majority of Swedish restaurant and kitchen managers.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation is largely managerial and knowledge-based: setting goals and strategies, creating guidelines, monitoring and evaluating operations, budgeting, and allocating resources. scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For hotell- och konferenschefer (SSYK 1710), there are clear current AI usage signals but they look like early-to-mid adoption rather than mainstream, occupation-wide deployment. Observable use cases in 2025/early 2026 likely include AI-assisted demand forecasting, pricing/revenue management support, staff scheduling, customer feedback analysis, marketing copy drafting, and meeting/conference planning support. However, these tools are more commonly embedded in broader hotel/revenue systems or used by adjacent functions than adopted as a standard, dedicated AI workflow for all managers. In Swedish hospitality, adoption is uneven across chains vs. independent hotels and is still constrained by legacy systems, limited budgets, and the operational/personnel-heavy nature of the role. Overall this fits 'some employers experimenting, tools exist but not mainstream' more than broad active adoption.

The most observable uses by 2025/early 2026 are primarily administrative: AI support for scheduling/staffing planning, documentation support, meeting and material summarization, simpler analysis of absenteeism/volumes, and sometimes AI in procurement or operational follow-ups. Some municipalities and private care providers are also testing decision support and language assistance in communication with staff and relatives. At the same time, the core operations of elderly care are heavily reliant on personnel and regulated, and the public sector often introduces AI more slowly. Thus, there are more than just isolated pilot signals, but adoption is not yet mainstream or broadly operationalized in the professional role. Therefore, the profession remains in the early experimental/limited implementation phase rather than a higher maturity level.

For this particular role, at the beginning of 2026, there are clear but still limited AI signals: generative tools are sometimes used for report writing, summaries, planning materials, meeting notes, and simpler analysis, and some larger agricultural/forestry companies are testing decision support systems connected to data from machines, weather, satellites, and field/forest inventories. At the same time, core tasks are strongly location- and operation-specific, often with low standardization, few clearly established AI workflows across the industry, and many decisions based on local experience, economics, and personnel management rather than automatable text or analysis production. scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Production managers in manufacturing primarily work with goals, strategies, budgets, resource allocation, monitoring, reporting, and negotiations. scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Manufacturing production managers have clear signals of AI adoption, but primarily at an early and selective level rather than as a standard tool in their roles. In Sweden, many manufacturing firms are actively piloting or rolling out AI in operations, and production leadership is a likely beneficiary because the role is closely tied to scheduling, resource allocation, and performance management. However, there is limited evidence that AI is yet a routine, role-specific core tool across the occupation; adoption varies widely by company size, digital maturity, and sector (especially between more advanced exporters and smaller plants). Thus, the best fit is early adoption with meaningful but not yet mainstream deployment.
